01-01:
Better to know a few things which are good and necessary than many things which are useless and mediocre. A small, selected library can be a great treasure, offering the wisdom of the wisest people from around the world. 

As Ralph Waldo Emerson notes, "A company of the wisest and the most deserving people from all the civilized countries of the world, for thousands of years, can make the results of their studies and their wisdom available to us." Lucius Annaeus Seneca advises, "Read only those books which are accepted without doubt as good." Henry David Thoreau also warns, "Read the best books first, otherwise you’ll find you do not have time."
01-02:
One of the worst prejudices known is held by the majority of the so-called scholars of our time, who claim that a person can live without faith. 

As GIUSEPPE MAZZINI notes, "Religion satisfies this requirement, and makes clear those connections which unite all people as brothers, revealing to them that they have the same source of origin, the same task for their lives, and the same general final goal."

At the heart of all religions lies a single unifying truth, as EWALD FLÜGEL states, "The general essence of all religions is love to your neighbor, and that this is requested by Manuf, Zoroaster, Buddha, Moses, Socrates, Jesus, Saint Paul, and Mohammed alike." The life of a person without faith is the life of an animal.
01-03:
The most important question to keep before ourselves at all times is this: Do we do the right thing? During this short period of time which we call our life, do our acts conform to the will of the force that sent us into the world? 

As John Ruskin said, "We have to fulfill honestly and irreproachably the work destined for us." When we remember that it is our duty to serve, our burden becomes lighter. Embracing this wisdom, that the meaning of life is to serve the force that sent us into the world, brings constant joy.
01-04:
We are connected to the rest of mankind by industry, trade, art, knowledge, and our common mortality. 

As the Chinese Proverb says, "Kind people help each other even without noticing that they are doing so, and evil people act against each other on purpose." 

We must support each other, as Muslih-ud-Din Saadi notes, "All the children of Adam are members of the same body. When one member suffers, all the others suffer as well." This interconnectedness is the foundation of our shared humanity.
01-05:
The damage done by our words can be invisible, but its impact is not diminished by our lack of awareness of those who suffer from them.

A gunshot wound may be cured, but the wound made by a tongue never heals. 
—PERSIAN WISDOM

If any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man…. Even so the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things. 
—JAMES 3:2, 5

I said, I will take heed to my ways, that I sin not with my tongue. 
—PSALMS 39:1 

Be cautious with your words, lest you destroy the unity of people by stirring bad feelings amongst them against another.
01-06:
It's essential to strive to abstain from evil, as this habit supports virtues and helps overcome challenges. 

As Lao-Tzu notes, "For he who is resolute in goodness, there is nothing that he could not overcome." 

Epictetus also advises, "Put at least half of your energy into making yourself free of empty wishes, and very soon you will see that in so doing you will receive much greater fulfillment and happiness." 

Additionally, the Dhammapada states, "I will call the right groom he who can stop his rage, which goes as fast as the fastest chariot." 

Ultimately, persistence is key, as every effort to overcome passions weakens their power and brings us closer to victory.
01-07:
Kindness is necessary in relationships with people. You have to respect every person, no matter how miserable or ridiculous he or she may be, for the same spirit that lives in us lives in them. 

As Arthur Schopenhauer said, "You should remember that in every person lives the same spirit which lives in us." The Book of Divine Thoughts also reminds us, "Do not be cruel of heart to those who are tempted, but try to console them, just as you would like to be comforted." 

The kinder and more thoughtful a person is, the more kindness he can find in other people, and kindness enriches our life, making difficult things easy and dull things cheerful.
01-08:
Christian teaching is so simple that even small children can understand its meaning, yet it's often obscured by those who claim to be Christians without truly embodying its principles.

As Buddha said, “A man who starts to live for his soul is like a man who brings a lantern into a dark house. The darkness disappears at once.” Christ's teachings are similarly distilled into a single, enduring commandment: “Love each other, as I loved you.” He emphasized love over faith, recognizing that love is eternal and unchanging. As one spiritual leader put it, "My religion is love to all living beings." To truly fulfill Christianity, we must strip away its perversions and restore its pure, loving essence.
01-09:
Knowledge is real knowledge only when it is acquired by the efforts of your intellect, not by memory. As Henry David Thoreau notes, "Only when we forget what we were taught do we start to have real knowledge." 

A constant flow of thoughts from others can stop and deaden your own thought, as Arthur Schopenhauer warns. He advises to "read less, study less, but think more" and only learn what you really need and want to know. This allows your own thoughts to flourish and truly influence your life.
01-10:
The basis of all education is the establishment of our relationship to the beginning of all things, and the conclusions about our behavior which may be drawn from this. 

But whosoever shall offend one of these little ones who believe in me, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea.
—MATTHEW 18:6-7

I think that the major obligation of parents and educators is to give children an understanding of the divine beginning that exists within them.
—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING
01-11:
Perfection is impossible without humility. The higher the position you occupy among other people, the more humble you should be. 

As the APOCRYPHA states, "More things have already been revealed to you than you can understand." This is echoed in the words of MATTHEW 20:28, "The Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life as ransom for many." 

Additionally, CONFUCIUS advises, "When an arrow does not hit its target, the marksman blames himself, not another person. A wise man behaves in the same way." Remembering past mistakes is also crucial, as it allows us to avoid repeating them and focus on doing good in the future.
01-12:
There are people who take responsibility for making decisions for others and determining their relationship to God and to the world; and there are people, the overwhelming majority of them, who give this authority to the others, and blindly believe in everything they are told. Both groups of people commit an equal crime.

As Ralph Waldo Emerson notes, we often blindly repeat the "truth" told to us by others, whether it's our grandmothers, teachers, or prominent people. 

The Bible warns, "Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep’s clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves. Ye shall know them by their fruits." Ultimately, every person should define for himself his attitude to this world and to God, using his intellect to test and discern the truth.
01-13:
Faith is the understanding of the meaning of life and the acceptance of those duties and responsibilities connected to it. A good man is one who has faith, with his will in consent with the world's conscience and wisdom.

—CHINESE PROVERB

We should give ourselves into the hands of God, allowing what should happen to happen, trusting that it will be good.

—HENRY AMIEL

Ultimately, faith brings peace, and without it, one cannot find peace for their soul. As Christ said, delivering one's spirit into God's hands is the key, and those who do so with all their heart will find their spirit in the best place, with nothing better.
01-14:
You should love only one thing in yourself, that which is the same in all of us. In loving that which is the same in all of us, you love God. 

As Jesus said, "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind" and "Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself." 

"I John" also reminds us, "God is love. And he who lives in love, lives in God, and God lives in him. If we love one another, God lives in us." Love is a consequence of understanding the divine, spiritual beginning that exists in all of us.
01-15:
The greatest truth of the teachings of Christ is the direct connection between a man (God’s son) and God (His Father). 

Christ's confidence in the greatness of the human soul was the most important feature of his character, as he saw in each person the reflection of God's image. 

As Ralph Waldo Emerson said, "Feel yourself talking directly to God: don’t read the book, read your soul; then a small chapel will become as big as the heavenly dome itself." 

Beware of everything which puts an obstacle between you and God.
01-16:
False faith is the major cause of most of our misfortunes. 

To succeed in bringing our life to a rational beginning, it's essential to see and study the irrational things in life and understand the possibility of a rational, wise life. As Henry George said, "A sailor who sets the same sails all the time, without making changes when the wind changes, will never reach his harbor." We should be ready to change our views and live with an open and receptive mind, accepting teachings like those of Christ, which reveal that "we live among big lies."
01-17:
Live for your soul, and without trying or even understanding that you’re doing it, you will contribute to the improvement of society. 

"God is your only Lord, your only master, and you do not need another. God will make you free." —F. ROBERT DE LAMENNAIS

To obtain wisdom, one must make an independent, serious effort of their own. As the Chinese Proverb states, "If you can teach a person kindness and love, but you do not, you lose a brother." Improve your own soul, and be confident that only in so doing can you contribute to the improvement of the larger society of which you are part.
01-18:
A scholar knows many books; a well-educated person has knowledge and skills; an enlightened person understands the meaning and purpose of his life. 

The only real science is the knowledge of how a person should live his life. As it stands, "we live a senseless life, contrary to the understanding of life by the wisest people of all times." This is because our young generations are educated in the wrong way, taught different sciences but not the meaning of life.
01-19:
Society can be improved only by self-sacrifice. 
Heaven and earth are eternal because they do not exist for themselves. 
—LAO-TZU
The improvement of man can be measured by the level of his inner freedom. The more a person becomes free from his personality, the more freedom he has.
01-20:
Death and birth are two limits, and something similar is hidden behind each. 

When a person leads a good life, he is happy at the present moment and he does not think what will happen after this life. 
"It is much better to believe that everything which God makes for us is good than to believe in all the pleasures of paradise."

When we die, our souls become free. 
A person should not think too much about what will happen after his life, but rather "follow the will of those who sent us into this world; that will is in our minds and our hearts."
01-21:
The more closely a person follows his intellect and controls his passions, the closer he comes to spiritual life, to love of God and of his neighbor. 

Instead of blaming the evil in others, we should focus on fighting the evil within ourselves, for it is possible to get rid of the evil inside of us. As Thomas Carlyle said, "Do not be embarrassed by your mistakes. Nothing can teach us better than our understanding of them." 

Our invisible work at the improvement of our soul is the most important work in the world, and all other visible kinds of work are useful only when we do this major work.
01-22:
No exceptions or special circumstances can justify the murder of a living person. As expressed in all religious teachings and people's conscience, murder is the most vulgar violation of the Law of God. 

The military, in time of peace, abides in complete idleness, lacking useful labor and obligations, instead creating notions of honor and subjugation. This way of life, which would bring shame to civilians, is boasted about by the military, who claim a right to a careless and joyful life due to their willingness to risk their lives at war.

The same divine beginning lives in all people, and no single person or gathering of people has the right to destroy this connection between the divine beginning and a human body, that is, to take a human life.
01-23:
Among all sins, there is one which completely opposes the major blessing of human life, which is your love for your brother: there is no worse sin than to destroy this major joy of life, by feeling rage and hatred for your brother.

As Seneca said, "when you want to escape from your rage, when you feel that it grows, the best thing to do is to stop." 

Rage is most harmful for the man who experiences it, and "your enemy will pay you back with rage, will make you suffer, but the biggest damage to you will be caused by the rage and hatred existing in your heart" —DHAMMAPADA. Ultimately, "your rage cannot be justified by anything. The reason for your rage is always inside you."
01-24:
Nobody knows where the human race is going. The highest wisdom, then, is to know where you should go: toward perfection. 

A wise man seeks wisdom; a madman thinks that he has found it. 
—PERSIAN PROVERB

It is not the place we occupy which is important, but the direction in which we move.
—OLIVER WENDELL HOLMES
01-25:
There is a basic knowledge which is necessary for every human being; until a person has acquired this basic knowledge, all other kinds of knowledge will be harmful for him. 

Socrates noted that in good systems of education, there is a certain limit you should not go beyond, emphasizing that sophisticated knowledge can distract from the most important human pursuit: moral perfection.

—XENOPHON

"Divert your gaze from the world of lies. Do not trust your feelings. Only in yourself, only in your impersonal self, can you find the eternal."
—DHAMMAPADA, a book of BUDDHIST WISDOM 

It is better to know several basic rules of life than to study many unnecessary sciences, as the latter may lead to pride and hinder understanding of the fundamental principles.
01-26:
A rich man cannot be merciful, for if he becomes truly merciful, he will quickly lose his riches. As Saint John Chrysostom notes, "Stop robbing others before you give money to beggars. With the same hand that we rob one person, we reward another, giving to the poor the money which we have taken from the even poorer."

The Bible also warns against empty charity, saying, "whoso hath this world’s good, and seeth his brother have need, and shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him, how dwelleth the love of God in him? My little children, let us not love in word; neither in tongue; but in deed and truth" (I John 3:17-18). True love and compassion require action, not just words.
01-27:
Love for other people gives a real feeling of good, and it unites you with other people and with God. 

A wise man loves not because he wants to profit from it but because he finds bliss in love itself. 

Just as a mother puts her life at risk to guard and save her only child, so every person should guard and save in himself love for every living being. 

The fearlessness, calm, inner peace, and joy which are given to us by love are so big that all other things in the world cannot be compared with them.
01-28:
For a person to know the law that makes him free, he must be elevated from a material to a spiritual life. 

As Jesus said, "When ye have lifted up the Son of man, then shall ye know that I am he, and that I do nothing of myself; but as my Father hath taught me, I speak these things." 

Submission to the law created by men makes one a slave; obedience to the law created by God makes one free. We live a short period of time in this world, but we live it according to the laws of eternal life.
01-29:
Wisdom is knowing the purpose of life, and knowing how to achieve it. You can achieve wisdom in three ways: the way of meditation, the way of imitation, and the way of experience.

—CONFUCIUS

The merit of a man is not in the knowledge he possesses, but in the effort he made to achieve it.

—GOTTHOLD EPHRAIM LESSING

Your soul is both your judge and your place of sanctuary. Your own soul is your highest judge.

—MANU
01-30:
When Socrates was asked where he came from, he said that he was a citizen of the world. He regarded himself as a citizen of the universe. 
—MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO

You should respond with kindness toward evil done to you, and you will destroy in an evil person that pleasure which he derives from evil. 
The true teaching of love is strong; it kills evil before evil can grow and become powerful.
01-31:
Only greatly insolent people establish a religious law to be taken for granted by others without discussion or doubts. As Heinrich Heine notes, "Many scoundrels try to disguise their dirty deeds by their dedication to the interests of religion, or by their high morals, or by love for their motherland." 

In Matthew 23:8-10, it is written, "But be not yet called Rabbi: for one is your Master, even Christ; and all ye are brethren. And call no man your father upon the earth: for one is your Father, which is in heaven. Neither be ye called masters: for one is your Master, even Christ." The essence of Christ's teaching is to follow his commandments, not just repeat empty words.
02-01:
The origin of the spiritual cannot be explained by the material. A man consists of body and soul, and it is the soul that is the most essential part. As Marcus Aurelius said, "You must learn this over time, and remember that your real life is in your spirit, that is, in your soul." 

Belief in a spiritual realm divides people into two groups: the living and the dead. An unbeliever focuses solely on material possessions and outer appearances, becoming "a liar, a snob, a slave" as Alexandr Arkhangelksy noted. In contrast, acknowledging the spiritual realm brings freedom, truth, and love, and is the key to a happy and fulfilling life.
02-02:
There are two different states of human existence: first, to live without thinking of death; second, to live with the thought that you approach death with every hour of your life. 

The more you transform your life from the material to the spiritual domain, the less you become afraid of death. 

When faced with doubts, imagining one's own mortality can bring clarity, as a man condemned to immediate execution will not think about material gains, but about consoling an abused person, helping an old person, or repairing a toy for a child.
02-03:
Kindness is for your soul as health is for your body: you do not notice it when you have it. 

A person becomes happy to the same extent to which he or she gives happiness to other people. 
—JEREMY BENTHAM

The will of God for us is to live in happiness and to take an interest in the lives of others. 
—JOHN RUSKIN

Nothing can make our life, or the lives of other people, more beautiful than perpetual kindness.
02-04:
A man is free only when he lives in truth, and the truth may be perceived only by the intellect. For a wise man, the wealth, the glory, and the rewards of this world are like sweets or empty shells on a road. 

—EPICTETUS

A distinctive feature of a thinking person is submission to his fate, as opposed to the shameful struggle with it that is a characteristic feature of animals.

—MARCUS AURELIUS

We can be free only through our intellect, for we are not free in this world, subdued by our passions and the emotions of other people.
02-05:
All events, whether in the lives of human individuals, or human societies, have their beginnings in thought. 

Our thoughts, depending on whether they are good or bad, can bring us either to paradise or to hell; this happens, neither in heaven nor under the earth, but here, in this life.
—LUCY MALORY

In order to change the nature of things, either within yourself or in others, one should change, not the events, but those thoughts which created those events.
02-06:
Sexual desire is the most all-consuming of desires, never sated, for the more it is satisfied, the more it grows.

People often take pride in controlling their desires, but this is a delusion. Remember how passionately you yearned in the past for things you now hate or despise. Try to tame your present desires, calm them; this is most beneficial, and most achievable.
02-07:
Perfecting the self is both an inner and an outer work: You cannot improve yourself without communicating with other people, influencing them, and being influenced by them. 

"Be perfect, as your heavenly Father," Christ said. This does not mean that Christ asked people to be as good as God; only that everyone should strive for perfection. 

There is nothing more harmful to you than improving only your material, animal side of life. There is nothing more beneficial, both for you and for others, than activity directed to the improvement of your soul.
02-08:
He who casts blame on another person is quick to think that he would not do the very same thing. When two people have a dispute, both are to blame, and it will stop only when at least one person understands their own guilt. 

"Judge not, that ye be not judged. For with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again."
—MATTHEW 7:1-2

Stop blaming other people, and you will feel relief in your soul, much like an alcoholic or smoker feels when they stop their habit.
02-09:
The material evil caused by war is big, but it is incomparably small in comparison with the perversion or the understanding of good and bad which happens during the war. 

"The most powerful weapon known is the weapon of blessing. Therefore, a clever person relies on it. He wins with peace, not with war." —LAO-TZU

War creates a state where power and glory is often received by the most undeserving and vicious people, while those who create hatred between nations commit a terrible crime.
02-10:
The higher the opinion a person has of himself, the more unstable is his position. 

To be strong, you have to be like water: if there are no obstacles, it flows; if there is an obstacle it stops; if a dam is broken, then it flows further; if a vessel is square, then it has a square form; if a vessel is round, then it has a round form. 
—LAO-TZU

The more a person analyzes his inner self, the more insignificant he seems to himself. This is the first lesson of wisdom. 
—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

Water does not stay on a mountaintop, but flows into the valley. In the same manner, real virtue does not remain with those people who want to be higher than the others; but virtue stays only with people who are humble.
—After the TALMUD
02-11:
A person’s life is good only to the extent that it fulfills the expectations of the will of God. Evil, in the form of suffering and death, is everywhere apparent, but only when a man lowers himself to the state of an animal does death and suffering scare him. The road to escape this fear is the road of fulfillment of the law of God, expressed in love.

"Be as you are, as you have to be, and the rest is God’s business." —HENRI AMIEL

We know the law of God, and our conscience can understand its applications to our life, for all real good stems from its requirements.
02-12:
We live our lives as if there will be no death, despite its inevitability. 

If only God is among us, and eternity exists, then everything is different. 
—ERASMUS

The more deeply you understand life, the less you grieve over the destruction caused by death. 
Whatever name you give to the origin of man, this spiritual quality of humans to understand, feel, and exist, it is holy, it is divine, and, therefore, it should be eternal.
—MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO
02-13:
Religion is a philosophy which can be understood by anyone. 

A person can please God only with the godliness of his life. If an outwardly faithful person is not good, clear, and humble in his life, then he presents a big lie, he serves God falsely.

—After IMMANUEL KANT

Religion is simple wisdom which is directed to the heart and understood by the intellect. Religion can enlighten philosophical meditations; philosophical meditations can strengthen religion.
02-14:
A holy spirit lives within you. 

Jesus answered and said unto him, Verily, verily, I say unto thee, Except a man be born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God.

—JOHN 3:3

Intellect can be enlightened only in a kind person. A person can be kind only when he has an enlightened intellect. One helps the other.

—CHINESE WISDOM

Even if people do not know what real kindness is, they nevertheless have it within them.

—CONFUCIUS 

God lives in every one of us. When a person remembers this, the thought of it can save him from evil, and help him to do good.
02-15:
The greatest truth is the most simple one. 

Simplicity is the consequence of refined emotions. 
—JEAN D’ALEMBERT

Good speech is always clear, clever, and understood by all. Words can unite people, and nothing can unite people more than truth and simplicity.
02-16:
The younger and more primitive a person is, the more they believe life is material and exists only in the body, but with age and wisdom, they come to understand that all life originates from the spirit.

"Look at the sky, and at the earth, and think that all things pass... Then you will understand the truth; you will see what remains, what does not pass." —BUDDHIST WISDOM

"Remember that you are not mortal; only your body is mortal. What is alive is not your body but the spirit living in your body." —After MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO 

A person can understand their real destination in life only after liberating themselves from the sensual, material world.
02-17: All people of the world have an equal right to the privileges of this world. Equality cannot be reached only by civil measures, but by love of God and people, which can be achieved through spiritual learning. As Lichtenberg said, "precisely because some people are stronger and smarter than others, that the principle of equality is necessary." Christ revealed that people are equal because the same spirit lives in all of them, and we should "learn from the small children, behave like children, and treat all people on an equal basis, with love and tenderness."
02-18:
Personality is a mask which disguises the divine being that lives in every person. The more one rejects personality, the more this divine beginning is manifested.

"You must love only God, and you must hate only yourself." —BLAISE PASCAL

"He who wants to save his soul, will lose it; and he who wants to lose his life for me and the new testament, will save it." —MARK 8:35

"Only those people who deny their own personality can understand the religious teaching." —The TALMUD

Only when we forget about ourselves can we truly connect with others and understand the truth of life, as those who do not see the meaning of their life in temporary things know the truth of life.
02-19:
It is a sin not to be engaged in work, even if it is not necessary for you to make your living with everyday work. 

One of the best and purest joys is having a rest after labor. 
—IMMANUEL KANT

Nothing can make a person feel more noble than work. Without work, a person cannot have human dignity. 

When we accept truth and repent our sins, we understand that nobody can have special rights, advantages, or privileges in this life, and our first obligation is to fight with nature for our life and for the lives of other people.
02-20:
Humankind goes on without stopping, and this movement forward is necessary for individual spiritual progress. 

The way of life of any people depends on their faith, which becomes simpler, clearer, and closer to the truth over time. As faith simplifies and clarifies, people become more united.

—After JOHN MILTON

Mankind makes progress when its faith progresses, and real religious progress comes from purifying revealed truths, not discovering something new. We should work toward religious progress, fighting prejudice and seeking a better understanding of pure religion, rather than substituting it with technical, scholarly, or artistic achievements.
02-21:
The time will come when more and more people will drop the terrible habit of eating animals. 

It is a mistake to believe that we have no moral responsibilities to animals, as this way lies complete vulgarity and rudeness. 
—ARTHUR SCHOPENHAUER

It is written in our own hearts more clearly than in any books—that we should take pity on animals in the same way as we do on each other.
02-22:
All that is written about God, and all that people say about Him, still falls short of the mark. 

As Lao-Tzu notes, "An intellect which can be understood is not an eternal intellect; a being which can be named is not an eternal being." Similarly, "God is the limitless being which accepts only truth," according to Matthew Arnold. 

Indian Wisdom reminds us, "Those people who ask where God is are crazy; God is everywhere, in all of nature and in the soul of each person." And Angelus Silesius cautions, "If your eyes become blinded by the sun, you do not say that the sun does not exist. In the same way, you should not say that God does not exist if your intellect is lost in trying to understand him."
02-23:
The existing design of life corresponds neither to the requirements of conscience nor to the requirements of intellect.

As William Paley notes, "Imagine a flock of pigeons... collecting all they can into one big heap... and then punish[ing] a weak pigeon who dares to take one grain from the heap without permission" — a stark illustration of mankind's day-to-day behavior.

Theognis laments, "I see people arguing with each other, preparing different traps for each other, lying and betraying each other. I cannot see without tears that the foundations of Good and Evil are forgotten, or in some cases completely unknown." This highlights the disconnect between human rationality and the prevalence of violence over reason in interactions.
02-24: For a truth to be heard, it must be spoken with kindness. As Henry David Thoreau said, "Only the words of a loving man can be heard." To speak the truth effectively, one must practice kindness and sincerity in their words. François de La Rochefoucauld notes, "We lie to other people so often that we get used to it, and we start to lie to ourselves," highlighting the importance of honesty in all interactions. Ultimately, truth telling is marked by humility and simplicity, rather than unkindness or self-assurance.
02-25:
To pray is to accept and remember the laws of the limitless being, God, and to measure all your deeds according to His laws. 

As the Talmud notes, "Those who make a habit out of prayer do not pray sincerely." Jean Jacques Rousseau adds, "If you ask for support from God, then you will learn how to find it in yourself. He does not change us, but we change ourselves by getting closer to Him."

During prayer, it's essential to "forget about all external worldly things and address the divine part of your soul," using this divine part to gain communication with that of which it is a part.
02-26:
Stop and reflect on your conversations, and you may find that many discussions were meaningless, empty, and trivial. 

Only speak when your words are better than your silence, as the Arabic Proverb advises. A stupid person should keep silent, notes Muslih-ud-Din Saadi, but if he knew this, he would not be stupid. 

Kind people avoid arguments, and truthful words are not always pleasant, observes Lao-Tzu. Before speaking, consider whether your words are necessary and if they might harm anyone.
02-27:
A charity is only then a real charity when it involves sacrifice. 

As James 5:3 warns, "Your gold and silver is cankered; and the rust of them shall be a witness against you, and shall eat your flesh as it were fire." A truly kind person cannot be rich, as a Chinese Proverb states, "A rich person, without question, is not a kind one." Jesus also taught, "Verily I say unto you, that a rich man shall hardly enter into the kingdom of heaven... it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God" (Matthew 19:23-24).
02-28:
Art is one of the means of unifying people. If beautiful art does not express moral ideas, ideas which unite people, then it is not art, but only entertainment. 

—IMMANUEL KANT

An artist is one of two things: he is either a high priest, or a more or less smart entertainer.

—GIUSEPPE MAZZINI

Meditations or discussions about art are the most useless pastimes known, as those who really know art understand that it can speak well with its own language.
02-29:
To move, you must know where to go, in terms both of everyday motion and of your whole life. 

"Perfection is of God. To wish for perfection is of man." 
—JOHANN WOLFGANG VON GOETHE

"Life is movement, a walk along the way of life to the fulfillment of those ideas which illuminate us, both in our intellect and in our hearts, with divine light." 
—After GIUSEPPE MAZZINI

"The ideal is within you, and the obstacle to reaching this ideal is also within you." 
—THOMAS CARLYLE
03-01:
The fear of death in man is the understanding of his sins. 

As Socrates said, "death is the state in which we abide during our sleep made permanent," a state we all know and find nothing terrible in. A spiritual person views death as setting the spirit free from the body, and knows the things that truly matter cannot be destroyed. 

We should prepare for death by living a good life, for it is inevitable. If you live a good life, you should not be afraid of death.
03-02:
The more closely a person unites with the will of God, the firmer this person becomes in his actions. To navigate life's troubles, we need a reliable escort, and the only real friend is God. If you follow God everywhere, you will steer clear of trouble, by wanting what He wants and not wanting what He does not want.

As Epictetus and Henri Amiel advise:
—After EPICTETUS
—HENRI AMIEL: Do not wish for too much, nor think that the things you wish for are the only right or necessary things. You should wish only for those things for which God wishes. 

The right path in life is very narrow, but it is crucial to find it to avoid plunging into the swamp of misunderstanding and evil.
03-03:
The reward for a good deed should be the joy it brings, and seeking other rewards lessens this feeling. 

He who does good to others makes the biggest gift to himself. 
—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

If you do good and ask for a reward, you weaken the force of your goodness. 
—From the BOOK OF DIVINE THOUGHTS

Do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing. 
—MATTHEW 6:3
03-04:
Eating to excess is a vice just as bad as many others. One of the most common sins against yourself is gluttony. As Socrates said, food should serve you in fighting your hunger, and not in developing sophisticated tastes. 

God gave food to people, and the devil gave cooks. Socrates reminded his students about the wise Odysseus, whom Circe, an evil sorceress, could not subject to her magic, because he did not eat to excess. 

Eating to excess is not considered to be a sin by many, because it produces no noticeable harm, but it is a sin that destroys human dignity.
03-05:
Just as it’s folly to try to lift yourself into the air, you should not praise yourself too much. When you praise yourself, you produce the opposite effect in others, and appear lower in their eyes.

—BLAISE PASCAL

A man who praises himself does not see anything except himself around him. 
He who always listens to what other people say about him will never find inner peace. 
A flatterer speaks his flattery because he has a low opinion of himself and of others, notes Jean De La Bruyère. If you want to preserve your good name, do not praise yourself and do not so much as allow others to praise you.
03-06: Love of God is love in itself; it is love for the sake of love. This kind of love is the highest blessing. As it's said, "God is love," and how can you be afraid of love? You should not fear God, but love Him. If you love God, then you will not be afraid of Him, or anything else in the world.
03-07:
Physical work, physical exercise for your body, is a necessary condition of life. As John Ruskin notes, "Work, the process of work in itself, is the most important thing for us, and its reward should be of minor importance; if it is, you please your creator, God." 

A Chinese man's response to mechanized labor highlights the value of physical work: "Physical work is good. Being relieved from physical work will be an enormous disaster." The Talmud also emphasizes the importance of physical labor, stating, "Every physical labor makes a man more noble. If you do not teach your son some physical skills, you teach him to rob others." Ultimately, doing good physical work is the best way to serve others and find joy and satisfaction.
03-08:
Prayer is a time to remind yourself of your attitude to limitless things, to God. 

Through prayer, you establish your attitude to the beginning of everything; you clarify your attitude toward people, your relationship with and your duties to them, and to the Father of all of us.

—"After the TALMUD"

Prayer is just a reminder for you of who you are and what the purpose of your life is. You will please God by submitting to Him.
03-09: War and Christianity are not compatible. War is one of the worst, most terrible things in this world. As the people come to understand the true nature of war, they will stop obeying orders, and it is this collective action that will ultimately bring an end to war, for "War will stop the moment the people who suffer from war fully understand that it is evil."
03-10:
That which gives life is the same in all things. All living creatures fear pain and death, and want what you want - to praise their lives. 

As the Dhammapada states, "All living creatures fear pain and death. Try to understand yourself in every living creature: do not torture and do not kill." 

We are all members of one great body, created from the same material, for the same purpose, as Lucius Annaeus Seneca notes, "Nature created us related to each other, from the same material, for the same purpose." 

Expunge from yourself anything which interferes with your feeling of a special connection between yourself and all living creatures.
03-11:
Marriage is necessary for the life of humanity, just as food is necessary for the life of an individual. Excess in marriage and sexuality creates evil for both individuals and society. 

As George Eliot notes, "It is a great thing when two souls are united to support each other in their work, in their successes and misfortunes, until the last silent minutes of the last good-bye." The union of a man and woman is a sacred bond, as stated in Matthew 19:3-6, "What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder." This unification is a great deed, not to be taken lightly or done as one pleases, but rather in accordance with the wisdom of those who have come before us.
03-12:
The deeds of a person become his life, become his fate. This is the law of our life. 

A Persian fable tells of a soul meeting a terrible woman after death, who reveals herself as the soul's deeds. 

As the Talmud and Indian Wisdom remind us: 
"Never postpone a good deed which you can do now, because death does not choose whether you have or haven’t done the things you should have done." 
"When you appeared in this world, you cried, and all the people around you rejoiced. You have to live your life in such a way that when you leave this world, you will rejoice, and all the people around you will cry." 

Your past deeds weigh heavily on the future direction of your life, but the effort of your spirit can sometimes change this direction.
03-13:
The condition of wisdom is purity; the consequence of wisdom is the peace of your soul. 

A wise person never considers himself to be wise. 
— 
Those people who have nothing to lose are very rich.
—CHINESE PROVERB 
Wisdom is limitless, and the closer you approach it, the more important it becomes for your life.
03-14:
Love brings people to unification, supported by the universal intellect that is the same for everyone. As Blaise Pascal notes, people often focus on trivial matters, neglecting what it means to be human. 

A person's major responsibility is to "make that intellectual spark which you have received from heaven illuminate the world around you," as stated in Chinese Wisdom. Everything in the world is a manifestation of the divine intellect, and all knowledge comes through the intellect.
03-15:
Be filled with love for other people, including those who are unpleasant or hostile to you. If you love your enemies, you will have no enemies. 

The most perfect among men is he who loves his neighbor without thinking about whether the person is good or bad. 
—MOHAMMED

Every time you are abused by someone and feel animosity toward him, remember that all people are children of God. Regardless of whether this person is unpleasant to you, you should not stop loving him as your brother, because, as much as you are, he is God’s son.
03-16:
Modern science cannot study everything; without being supported by religion, science does not know what it should study. Real wisdom is not the knowledge of everything, but the knowledge of which things in life are necessary, which are less necessary, and which are completely unnecessary to know. 

As Jean Jacques Rousseau said, "Among the most necessary knowledge is the knowledge of how to live well, that is, how to produce the least possible evil and the greatest goodness in one’s life." 

A person who knows much mostly keeps silent, because he knows that there is much more he doesn’t know. As a result, he speaks only when it is necessary to speak. It's essential to be strict in selecting the knowledge you want to acquire, as not all knowledge is good, and many false meditations are disguised as useful knowledge.
03-17:
We can improve this world only by distributing the true faith among the world’s people. 
—GIUSEPPE MAZZINI

A society cannot live without a united faith and purpose. 
The apostles lived united, in such a way that they shared one heart and soul. 
—After SAINT JOHN CHRYSOSTOM

Christianity in its pure and sincere form works like dynamite: it blows up old mountains and opens up new, limitless horizons. 
If you see that some aspect of your society is bad, and you want to improve it, there is only one way to do so: you have to improve people, by becoming better yourself.
03-18:
Blaming other people is always wrong, because no one knows what has happened and happens in the soul of another person. We often judge others, but a person changes constantly, like a river, and every new day differs from the last. 

As the Talmud says, "Never blame your neighbor until you have been in his place." Additionally, Publilius Syrus advises, "Forgive other people for many things, but do not forgive yourself anything." Recognizing that people often do evil because they cannot restrain themselves, we should refrain from blaming them, just as we acknowledge our own struggles with self-control.
03-19:
The creation of the world would have been a very bad act were it right for rich people to live off the work of the poor, and yet think that they were the benefactors.

"A stone falls on a pot—woe to the pot; a pot falls on a stone—woe to the pot; in every case, it is bad for the pot." —The TALMUD
"The pleasures of the rich are often acquired by the tears of the poor." 

Wealth is created by the concentration of human labor, with one people producing labor and others concentrating it, highlighting the imbalance in the system. The rich, fed and dressed by the work of the poor, live in luxury created for them by the poor, revealing a profound flaw in the world's design.
03-20:
Those who live according to the will of God cannot be sensitive to the judgments of other people. 

You should think in such a way that everybody could look into your soul and see what is transpiring there.
—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

To feel shame in the company of other people is good, but it is even better to experience shame when you are alone with yourself. You can hide some things from other people, but you cannot hide anything from God. 
For nothing is secret, that shall not be made manifest; neither any thing hid, that shall not be known and come to light.
—LUKE 8:17
03-21:
We know our life as it exists only here, in this world; therefore, if our life is to have any meaning, it should be here in this world.

"Do not wish for death just because your life is hard. All the burdens on your shoulders will help you fulfill your destiny." 
—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

"Life is neither suffering nor pleasure, but the business which we have to do, and which we have to finish honestly, up to our life’s end." 
—ALEXIS DE TOCQUEVILLE

This world is the place of our work, and all our forces, all our efforts, should be directed toward this life.
03-22:
If truth makes our life easier, then it is better to accept the truth than to hide from it. We should live our lives as if everyone could see us, as if the most secret corners of our soul were open to the sight of others. As Lucius Annaeus Seneca implies, all divine and human learning can be summarized in one truth—that we are members of one big body. 

"Seek the truth: it always shows us what we should do, what we should not do, and what we should stop doing."
03-23:
The earth, the air, and the sun belong to all of us; they cannot be made objects of property. 

As F. Robert de LaMennais notes, "We are all visitors in this world. Wherever you go in this world... there will always be a man waiting to tell you, 'This is my property. Get out of here!'" Henry George further emphasizes the issue of land ownership, stating, "If you leave a man on land which is someone else’s property and tell him that he is a completely free man and can work for himself, it’s as if you drop him in the middle of the Atlantic and tell him that he is free to go ashore." This highlights the problem of treating land as a private possession, rather than a shared resource, leading to the conclusion that those who own excessive land can be seen as contributing to the poverty of others.
03-24:
The person who fulfills God's law can understand God, and the more closely he fulfills it, the better he understands. As Arabic Wisdom notes, seeking God is a process with limitless sides and aspects.

Moments of hesitation about God's existence can lead to a better understanding of Him. A wise man, when asked how he knew God existed, replied, "Is it necessary to have a torch in order to see the sun?" This response highlights the inherent knowledge of God's existence, beyond words. Additionally, as Moses was told, "If you are looking for me, you have already found me," suggesting that the act of seeking is itself a form of discovery.
03-25:
As you acquire objects, and you use them, you should keep in mind that they are the products of people’s work. 

"A strong and wise person uses his gifts to support other people." —JOHN RUSKIN

Help should be mutual, with those who accept help paying back not only with money, but with love, respect, and gratitude.
03-26:
The most important change that can occur in a person’s life is a change in his faith. 

As Giuseppe Mazzini notes, "We are temporary visitors in this world; after we are educated, we are called to different places, and we pass away. But the general education of mankind goes on, very slowly but without interruption." It is a big mistake to think that faith is immutable, that it does not change over generations. The longer mankind exists, its faith becomes simpler and stronger.
03-27:
The more a person believes in God, the less he is afraid of other people. 

Do not despair if you see that you cannot accomplish all the good which you would like to accomplish. 
—MARCUS AURELIUS

Those who fear people do not fear God; those who fear God do not fear people. He who is not afraid of anything, and who is ready to give his life for a righteous cause, is much stronger than he whom other people fear. 

You want to save yourself from the power of other people—give yourself to the power of God. If you see yourself as being in the power of God, then people cannot do anything to you.
03-28:
Wisdom can be achieved by inner work, through solitary communication with yourself; it also can be achieved when you communicate with other people. 

Listen and be attentive, but do not speak too much; and when you are asked a question, answer briefly. 
—I have learned many things from my teachers; I have learned many things from my friends; and I have learned even more from my students.
—The TALMUD

Real love is not in words but in deeds, and only love can give you real wisdom. When you are in company, do not forget what you have found out when you were thinking in solitude; and when you are meditating in solitude, think about what you found out by communicating with other people.
03-29:
If you feel that your passions have gained victory over you, do not think that you cannot conquer them at all. A victory over oneself is a bigger and a better victory than a victory over thousands of people in a score of battles. 

"A passion in a person’s heart is like a spider’s web. At the beginning it is an alien visitor; then it becomes a regular guest; then it becomes master of the house." —After the TALMUD

"He who has achieved victory over himself has real power." —EASTERN WISDOM

Abstention cannot be achieved at once, but it should be a process, and a constant effort, with time and persistence helping you in these efforts to master your passions.
03-30:
The truth is not only a joy, it is an instrument in conflict much more powerful than violence.

Jesus teaches us about the power of forgiveness, saying, "I say not unto thee, Until seven times: but, Until seventy times seven" (Matthew 18:22). As Epictetus advises, when sharing truth with others, "do not be irritated, and do not say unkind or abusive words." Instead, Marcus Aurelius suggests correcting others "in a humble way," and if they do not listen, to "continue to be humble" and not place blame.
03-31:
To repent means to show your vices and weaknesses to all, taking responsibility for your mistakes and purifying your soul. 

A kind man who doesn't accept his mistakes and tries to justify himself becomes unkind. As the Talmud notes, "Nothing can make a person’s soul softer than the understanding of his own blame, and nothing can make one harder than the desire always to be right."

The Dhammapada also reminds us, "Those who cover their old sins with present-day good deeds remind me of the moonlight which illuminates this dark world on a cloudy night." Recognizing our flaws and limitations is essential for growth and self-improvement.
04-01:
The most critical piece of knowledge is the knowledge of what is essential to learn and what isn’t. 

As Immanuel Kant notes, "We have vast treasures of knowledge at our disposal but after we study them, we often do not use them at all." George Lichtenberg also warns, "Too-voracious reading, begun at too early an age, fills our minds with undigested material." Furthermore, it's essential to "Beware of false knowledge. All evil comes from it."
04-02:
The only real life is one lived close to God, and this requires effort to achieve. 

As Immanuel Kant notes, "Real good is achieved only with effort," for even good deeds become devoid of virtue when they become habits. 

Marcus Aurelius advises embracing life's challenges, saying, "When you carry your burden, you should know that it is good for you to have it." 

Spiritual effort and the joy that comes from understanding life are intertwined, much like physical exertion and rest, and it is through this effort that we can gain a deeper, more joyful understanding of life.
04-03:
When I die, either my essence will transform into a different being, or I will stop being a separate individual and become a part of God. Either possibility is good. 

As Marcus Tullius Cicero notes, "Wise people willingly submit to this order" of life, with its natural beginning, life, and ending. Ralph Waldo Emerson's insight also resonates: "All that I know about God brings me to the following conclusion: all that He did for us were the best things possible." You should live your life so that you are not afraid of death, and at the same time do not wish to die.
04-04:
Life should and can be limitless joy. Life could be limitless joy, if we would only take it for what it is, in the way it is given to us. 

A friendly mood and loving attitude are as an oil which lubricates the wheels of life, making them move easily and smoothly. 
—MARCUS AURELIUS

The secret of happiness? Enjoy small pleasures.
—SAMUEL SMILES

A truly wise man is always joyful. The best way to live joyfully is to believe that life was given for joy. When joy disappears, look for your mistake.
04-05:
It is difficult to avoid working in life without either sinning, committing violence, being a party to violence, or by flattering and pleasing the agents of violence.

Being poor is better than living in luxury and serving the rich. 
—INDIAN PROVERB

A dress presented to you as a gift by the king may be beautiful, but your own simple dress is better. 
—MUSLIH-UD-DlN SAADI

To those people who do not work the land, the soil says: if you do not work me, by applying physical labor with both of your hands, then you will stand in front of the doors of others asking for help; you will always be fated to use the leftovers of the rich.
—ZOROASTER
04-06:
The improvement of one's soul is the most important and necessary activity, encompassing all others. 

The biggest happiness is when at the end of the year you feel better than at the beginning.
—HENRY DAVID THOREAU

To strive for perfection, as the Father in heaven is perfect, requires finding the presence of the Holy Spirit within. This pursuit demands a balance between solitude, where one can meditate and reflect, and everyday life, where one can apply their growth.
04-07:
To repay evil with goodness is easier, wiser, and more natural than to repay evil with evil.

"Conquer rage with humility, conquer evil with goodness, conquer greed with generosity, and conquer lies with truth." 
—DHAMMAPADA, a book of BUDDHIST WISDOM

"When we treat our neighbors as they deserve to be treated, we make them even worse; when we treat them as if they were who we wish they were, we improve them." 
—JOHANN WOLFGANG VON GOETHE

Then said Jesus, Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do.
—LUKE 23:33-34
04-08:
People think that if they call mass murder “war,” then mass murder will stop being a murder, a crime.

As Theodore Parker notes, "one can deny Christ... when you call Him your master, and you claim to follow His commandments, but you suppress any free thought by quoting his words, and disguise all stupidities, all mistakes, and all sins of the people in his name." Alfred de Vigny also emphasizes, "It is not true that a war against a foreign nation can be sacred... War is cursed by God, as are those who participate in it." Ultimately, "your hands are stained with blood, your fingers with guilt; your lips speak falsehood, and your tongue utters deceit" (Isaiah 59:2-3), and murder is always a crime, no matter the justification.
04-09:
Love of goodness and faith in immortality are inseparable. Our beliefs are based not on logical proofs but on moral ones, and faith in God is closely connected with our nature. 

As Immanuel Kant said, "I can say that God exists and that my 'self' is immortal." This connection is deeply felt when "the animal beginning disappears, the veil is taken from the future, the darkness disperses" and we feel our immortality, as noted after Saint Martin. 

We are part of a larger, ongoing conversation with God, hearing only a small part of the "great speech" that started long before us and will continue after we're gone. If you truly love goodness, then you cannot have doubts in your immortality.
04-10:
The holy spirit which exists in people is liberated more and more, changing our existing world order. 

"The further any purpose the faster we should work toward it." —GIUSEPPE MAZZINI
"The longer I live, the more things I must complete." —WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

The more we understand our divine nature, the more its rules should be fulfilled in our actions.
04-11:
Everything is connected more closely in the spiritual world than in the material world. Every lie brings multiple lies, every cruelty brings more cruelty. 

As Zanizad Rafeazsky notes, "Very often people are proud of the purity of their conscience only because their memory is too short." 

Drop after drop, water fills the vessel; in the same way those who want to be good, become filled with goodness, as stated in the Dhammapada, a book of Buddhist wisdom. Blaise Pascal also observes, "Many of our vices exist only because they are supported by other vices; therefore, if we destroy our major vices, many others will disappear at once, in the same way as branches fall when you cut the trunk of a tree." 

Be attentive to the appearance of evil, and trust the inner voice that tells you about approaching evil, prompting you to stop, seek improvement, and defeat it.
04-12:
At a certain level of self-awareness, a person understands something supernatural in himself. 

"God exists because we exist. You can call it any other name, but there is no doubt that the superior life which created us exists." —GIUSEPPE MAZZINI

"God exists only for those who look for Him. Start looking: you will find Him in you and yourself in Him." 

Looking for God is a continuous process, and the moment one becomes satisfied, they lose Him. 

Just because a person does not understand God, he has no right to draw the conclusion that God does not exist.
04-13:
We understand the divine, spiritual beginning of our life both with our intellect and with our love. A wise man embodies three key qualities: he practices what he preaches, remains truthful, and is patient with others' weaknesses. 

As Luc de Vauvenargues notes, "Great thoughts come directly from the heart." This harmony between heart and intellect is essential, for "a clever person cannot be evil" and "a kind person is always clever." By cultivating kindness and intellect, we can deepen our understanding and connection to the divine.
04-14:
We cannot hope to obtain any sort of perfection in a society which is divided into two parts: rich people who rule the world and poor people who obey their orders. 

As Thomas Carlyle notes, "we say that we live in society but at the same time we live lonely lives." Henry George also observes, "If there are millionaires, there should be paupers." Additionally, Maimonides remarks, "If you have an income without working hard, then someone worked hard without receiving an income." Ultimately, excessive wealth is worse than poverty, and it is worse to be an oppressive master than an obedient slave.
04-15:
The consequences of our notions cannot be known to us, because they ripple outward limitlessly in a limitless world. 

Our efforts to penetrate the mystery of God are futile. It is enough to follow the divine law. 
—The TALMUD

A saint lives with his inner life; he denies outer life.
—LAO-TZU

Great deeds have very remote consequences.
—JOHN RUSKIN 

If you can see all of the consequences of your actions, then your actions are of no use.
04-16:
To accept the dignity of another person is an axiom. It has nothing to do with subduing, supporting, or giving charity to other people. 

The smallest detail can benefit the strengthening of character. 

A man is humble. He can hardly say: I exist, I can think. —After RALPH WALDO EMERSON

A person should know that he performs true charity not in front of other persons, but before the eternal law of God. Some religious people in Russia make a low bow to those they meet, acknowledging the divine spirit within each person, showcasing the depth of this foundation.
04-17:
Christianity is the study of the divine beginning in a person. It's a simple thing: love other persons, as you love God. 

—THEODORE PARKER

The essence of all religious teachings is love, with Christianity uniquely emphasizing nonresistance to evil and violence. To find strength and peace, work and improve your faith.
04-18:
What is important is not the quantity of your knowledge, but its quality. Ignorance in itself is neither shameful nor harmful, but pretending to know what you don't is both shameful and harmful. 

As Blaise Pascal notes, there are two types of ignorance: the pure, natural kind and the ignorance of the so-called wise, who often despise simple people and things. The truth should often overcome thousands of obstacles, until it is accepted, as George Lichtenberg says. Additionally, Eastern Wisdom teaches that a scholar who thinks but does not create is like a cloud that does not give rain.
04-19:
A man who does not understand the benefit of suffering does not live a clever and true life. 

Mankind has never achieved greatness but through suffering.
—F. ROBERT DE LAMENNAIS

Suffering brings a person closer to God, and seen in this light, it loses its bitter side and becomes bliss.
04-20:
For a person who leads a spiritual life, self-sacrifice brings a bliss that far transcends the pleasure of a person who lives by the self-indulgent satisfaction of his animal passions.

As Jean De La Bruyère notes, "He who is kind does good for other people. And if a person suffers while he does kind deeds, he becomes an even better person." Similarly, Matthew 10:37 reminds us, "He that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me: and he that loveth son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me." Lucy Malory also emphasizes the importance of charitable work, stating, "There is no higher blessing for a person than to do charitable work for the benefit and well-being of others." Ultimately, living for others allows us to transcend our own selfishness, as we find that "we live for ourselves only when we live for others."
04-21:
In the future, the order of the social life of the Christian world will be changed by the replacement of violence and its fears with love and blessing.

It is a mistake to think that there are times when you can safely address a person without love. You cannot work with people without love, being mindful of their humanity, just as you cannot work with bees without being cautious. 

—JOHN 15:17-19
—GOTTHOLD EPHRAIM LESSING 
You should live so that it is possible to create the kingdom of love on earth. You should live a life based not on violence but on love.
04-22:
The greatest knowledge is self-knowledge. He who understands himself will understand God. 

As Jesus said, "He that believeth on me, believeth not on me, but on him that sent me." This understanding is rooted in purity of soul, for "Without purity of soul, how can you say, I will glorify God?" —Wabana Purana, Indian Wisdom. By recognizing and embracing our spiritual and divine essence, we can transform our inner self, moving from a domain of suffering to one of steadiness and joy.
04-23: Real goodness is always simple. As Gregory Skovoroda notes, "Give thanks to God, who made necessary things simple, and complicated things unnecessary." Ralph Waldo Emerson also observes, "Most of our spending is done to forward our efforts to look like others." Great and true things are always simple and humble, done in a quiet way, without the need for pretenses or artificial displays.
04-24:
In any struggle, true bravery lies within those who know that God is their ally. As Jesus said, "These things I have spoken unto you, that in me ye might have peace. In the world ye shall have tribulation: but be of good cheer; I have overcome the world." —JOHN 16:33

Only virtue is clear and cannot be destroyed by any force, as Marcus Tullius Cicero noted, "Everything is indefinite, misty, and transient; only virtue is clear, and it cannot be destroyed by any force." A person who renounces their personality becomes truly powerful, allowing God to act through them. This is exemplified by Samuel, a Jewish rabbi, who returned the Roman empress's lost jewelry after thirty days, saying, "I wanted to show you that I returned your lost jewelry, not because of fear of your punishment, but because of fear of God."
04-25:
When you understand yourself as a spiritual being, then you are free. 

"Love of God" is the effort to add part of yourself to the higher creative flow of energy in this world. As Chinese Wisdom notes, "I know that the sky knows everything, and that its laws are constant." If a person does not believe himself capable of doing the best things in the world, then he starts to create the worst things. We should try to reflect this heavenly light, allowing it to guide us.
04-26:
The understanding of the existence of God is accessible to everybody; the complete understanding of the essence of God is not accessible to any person. 

As Jean Jacques Rousseau notes, "All the nations of the world name and respect God. Different people give him different names, and put different clothes on him; but there is only one God under all these different manifestations."

Faith in God is a fundamental aspect of human nature, as George Lichtenberg observes, "Faith in God is as natural to men as their ability to walk on two feet." Ultimately, the mystery of God's existence may be beyond human comprehension, as Blaise Pascal suggests, "There are some statements which cannot be fathomed: God exists or he does not exist..." 

Live in God, live together with God, by understanding Him in you, and do not try to define Him with words.
04-27:
A bad mood often leads to blaming others, but blaming others can also worsen our feelings. 

As Epictetus implies, people are not fixed entities, but rather complex and multifaceted. If there is animosity between two people, both are to blame. 

A person always tries to do what is best for himself, and if he is mistaken, suffering will follow. Remembering this can help us avoid being upset by others, reproaching them, or becoming their enemy. 

To cultivate understanding, consider making an agreement with those you live with to end arguments as soon as blame arises.
04-28:
Work is the necessary condition of happiness. 
"Manual labor does not exclude intellectual activity, but improves its quality and even helps it." —CHARLES DE MONTESQUIEU
"When a person sets to work, even if it is the most unqualified, primitive, simple work, the human soul calms down." —THOMAS CARLYLE
Work is necessary for a good disposition of the spirit, but it must be balanced, as excessive work or idleness can be destructive.
04-29:
A person can fulfill his purpose in life equally as well in illness as in health. 

If we meditate upon the existence of life after death, then all illnesses would seem to be taking us closer to the movement from one life to another. As we navigate these pains, we can prepare for the new state of our next existence. 

Christ served God and all mankind when he suffered on the cross, and even then he forgave those who killed him. Every person can do likewise. 

Illnesses almost always destroy one’s physical power, and they release the power of one’s soul. For a person who concentrates his consciousness in the spiritual domain, illnesses do not diminish his goodness, but on the contrary, they increase it.
04-30:
It seems that it is impossible to live without discovering the purpose of your life. The real purpose of our existence is to understand this limitless life existing in this world. 

A person may not know the purpose of his life, but he should know how to live. Every living being has sensory organs which reveal to it its place in the world. For a human, the primary sense is the intellect. 

If you do not know your place in the world and the meaning of your life, you should know that there is something to blame; and it is not the social system, or your intellect, but the way in which you directed your intellect.
05-01:
He who sees his life as a process of spiritual perfection does not fear external events. Abu Ganifakh, a famous spiritual teacher, demonstrated this when he refused to complain or seek revenge after being struck by a guard in prison, saying he would "enter paradise only with" his offender. 

The highest courage is the courage to be higher than your rage and to love a person who has offended you. As Epictetus advises, "Criticize yourself, but do not feel desperate about it." Jesus also taught, "fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to destroy both soul and body in hell."
05-02:
People very often do not accept the truth, because they do not like the form in which the truth is presented to them. 

"The beginning of a quarrel is like a flood which breaks through a dam. As soon as it rushes through, you cannot stop it." 
—The TALMUD

"During an argument, make your words very soft, and your heart hard. Do not try to lash out at your opponent, but try to convince him." 
—GEORGE WILKINS

If you know the truth, try to pass it on to others as simply as you can, along with the feeling of love for those persons to whom you pass it.
05-03:
The life of Jesus Christ is a profound example of a life that didn't see the fruits of his work during his lifetime. 

Clever people study to know more, undeserving people study to be more known, as Eastern Wisdom notes. 

To reach spiritual perfection, one must prioritize the purity of their soul, seeking truth and wholeness through true knowledge, as Confucius advises. 

Ultimately, every person's purpose is to find perfection in goodness, and only knowledge that leads to this is necessary.
05-04:
Every thought which is expressed by words is a force, and this force is limitless. 

"Good thoughts which originate from the hearts of men are as useful as good examples." 
—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

"A thought is an intellectual and vital energy of life, and it can be either a curse or a blessing, depending on its quality." 
—LUCY MALORY

Truth expressed in words is the greatest force there is in the lives of people, with consequences that are not immediately seen. Use good thoughts of wise people and refrain from distributing false ones.
05-05:
Religious teaching, that is, the explanation of the purpose and meaning of life, should be the basis of any education. 

You should always be truthful, especially with a child. You should always do what you have promised him, otherwise you will teach him to lie. 
—After the TALMUD

Man, whose teacher is nature, should not be a piece of wax on which an elevated image of some professor is to be carved.
—GEORGE LICHTENBERG

Do not say to a child whom you teach that there is a final truth, if you do not believe in something completely, or if you have some doubts. To do such a thing is a great crime.
05-06:
Compassion for animals is so natural to us that we can become insensitive to their suffering and death only through traditions or hypnosis. Compassion for animals comes from the same source as compassion toward people, and is closely connected with kindness - a person cannot be kind if he is cruel to animals.

—ARTHUR SCHOPENHAUER

A person is not higher than other animals because he can mercilessly torture them, but because he can take pity on them.

—BUDDHIST WISDOM

Do not allow your children to kill insects; this is the first step along a road which can lead to the killing of people.

—PYTHAGORAS
05-07:
A person who tries to find good outside himself, either in this life or in the one to come, is making a mistake. 

I went all over without rest, at night and during the day, and then I heard from a preacher, who opened the truth to me, that the answer is inside my soul, and that the light which I was looking for everywhere in the world is inside me.

—EASTERN WISDOM

You either make evil for yourself, or you escape sin and purify yourself; you are bad or evil, or you are pure; and nobody can save you except for yourself.

—DHAMMAPADA, a book of BUDDHIST WISDOM

Nothing can give a man strength when he is looking for salvation other than his own effort.
05-08:
There is nothing more attractive than humbleness and kindness. 

A wise man said to another man, “If you have any grievance against me, tell me now, before we enter the city, otherwise other people will hear it and they will attack you.” 
—EGYPTIAN WISDOM

But he that is greatest among you, let him be as the younger; and he that is chief, as he that doth serve.
—LUKE 22:26-27

The rivers and seas are the masters of the valleys across which they flow. This is because they are lower than the valleys.
—LAO-TZU

Try to avoid making judgments about yourself, especially by comparing yourself with others. Compare yourself only with perfection.
05-09:
Life is constant change; it ought to consist of weakening the material and increasing the spiritual side of our existence. 

Seek to learn constantly while you live; do not wait in the faith that old age by itself will bring wisdom, as Solon advises. Virtue always lasts longer than other qualities, and it always starts from the beginning, according to Immanuel Kant. 

The KORAN also reminds us that wherever we go, we should try to be kind and good, and that God tests us, but will ultimately unite us. Only spiritual things exist in reality, and the material world is only an illusion. To save our soul, we must forget about our comfort.
05-10:
Only those things which are spiritual are real, while the physical world is an illusion. 

As Nikolai Gogol notes, "Life is given to us not only for pleasures." This perspective is echoed in Matthew 6:24, "No man can serve two masters; for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon." Henri Amiel further emphasizes the importance of spiritual transformation, stating, "In the long run, there is only one subject worthy of study, and this is the different forms of transformation of the spirit." Ultimately, our thoughts and spirit can transcend physical boundaries, as Lucy Malory observes, "My thoughts by themselves are a spiritual power; they can exist at the same time in thousands of places."
05-11:
The moving force of all perfection, both for individuals and for whole nations, is not the understanding of what exists in this world, but the understanding of what can be achieved.

“You must be as perfect as your father in heaven.” This guiding principle underscores the pursuit of perfection as a fundamental aspect of human striving. The perfection of the highest kindness is the ultimate goal for all mankind.

No matter how low a person may fall, he will always be able to see that perfection toward which he should move, guided by inner direction and the pursuit of a higher ideal, much like a sailor navigates by the stars and compass when far from shore.
05-12:
Life is the constant approach to death; therefore, life can be bliss only when death does not seem to be an evil. A wise man, one who understands this, looks for the way which leads to calmness.

"A man comes into the world with his hands pushed into fists, as if he wants to say, 'All this world is mine.' A man leaves this world with his palms open, as if to say, 'Look, I take nothing with me.'" —The TALMUD

"You should live your life as if you are ready to say good-bye to it at any moment, as if the time left you is some pleasant surprise." —MARCUS AURELIUS

"Remember, you do not live in the world, you only pass through it."
05-13:
Every person should decide questions of life and death for himself. 

A wise man sets requirements only for himself; an unwise man makes requirements for others, as noted in Chinese Wisdom. 

Nothing will bring you peace except yourself, says Ralph Waldo Emerson. A soul does not learn; it simply remembers what it knew all the time, according to Daud El Gaffer. 

Ultimately, only the truth acquired by your own thinking belongs to us, as Arthur Schopenhauer states.
05-14:
The soul knows everything and is not afraid of anything, living according to its own laws, bigger than space and older than time. 

As Ralph Waldo Emerson says, "The soul is not afraid of anything. It lives according to its own laws. It is bigger than space and older than time." 

Epictetus adds, "Do you think that anybody can damage your soul? Then why are you so embarrassed? I laugh at those who think they can damage me." 

Ultimately, the most precious part of us cannot be damaged by anyone or anything, and we should fear nobody and nothing.
05-15:
The most common and the most widely used deceit is the wish to deceive not other people, but yourself. 

—JEAN JACQUES ROUSSEAU

People should follow only the indisputable truth.
—CONFUCIUS

Let us be truthful. This is the mystery of rhetoric and virtue, this is the biggest mystery, this is the highest achievement in art and the major law of life.
—HENRI AMIEL

One of the most common mistakes is to think that you can live without truth, as the inner and outer consequences of even the smallest lies are usually more harmful than those small unpleasant things which result from telling the truth directly.
05-16:
Humanity has never lived and cannot live without religion. The stronger the religious feeling is, the better one understands what is and what should be the guide of all one’s actions. 

As Henri Amiel notes, "it is dangerous to think that you are more clever than the majority of religious mankind." For one who destroys in himself his religious conscience, all of the religious activity of other people will produce a similar impression.

If a person has problems, there can only be one root cause: lack of faith. This truth also applies to human societies as a whole, highlighting the essential role of religion in guiding individuals and communities.
05-17:
The greatest joy is being able to endure everything, including suffering and slander, without feeling animosity, but instead feeling joy through faith. 

As Chinese Wisdom states, "The quality of a really virtuous person is to be unknown to people, or to be misunderstood by people, but not to be disappointed by this." Additionally, "When they blame you and scold you and discuss you, then you should rejoice; when they praise you and admire you, then you should be sad." This perspective suggests that criticism can be a valuable test of one's character and love for others.
05-18:
The understanding of the divine essence of your soul raises you to a level that transcends notions of force or weakness, and therefore of power. 

"Heaven is closer to those people who purify their souls." 
—INDIAN WISDOM

"Because he is in my heart." 
—PERSIAN WISDOM
