True understanding comes from looking at the essence of life with the eyes of your heart, and knowing God is essential to knowing yourself. If you are united with God, who can harm you, who can be more powerful than you?
05-19:
The foundation of all faiths is the same. 

A wise man was asked, “Is there a single word which you can follow throughout all your life?” And the wise man answered, “There is such a word. This is shu.” And the meaning of this word is, “If we do not want certain things to be done to us, we should not do such things to others.” 

—CHINESE WISDOM

You should behave in such a way that you can say to everybody, “Behave as I do.” 
—After IMMANUEL KANT 

When interacting with others, remember the law of reciprocity: do to others those things which you want done to you.
05-20:
For a person, for an animal, indeed for any living being, there is neither logic nor meaning in the word “freedom” because all our lives are limited by many constraints. However, if a person understands himself as a spiritual being, he cannot even speak about not being free. 

And ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free.

—JOHN 8:32

Evil does not exist in material nature by itself, but evil exists for every person who understands goodness, and who has the freedom of choice between good and bad.

—MARCUS AURELIUS

We understand very clearly that to be a person with high morals is to be a person with a liberated soul.
05-21:
In order to live in goodness, try doing it. Celebrate each new day with a good action, a good deed, as this is the best way to start a new day. 

—HARRIET MARTINEAU says, "Nobody has any idea about what goodness is, until they start doing it." 

—The TALMUD notes, "God’s love will go from heaven to those who give to the poor. A double blessing will go to those who, while doing this, meet the poor and tenderly say “good-bye” to them."

—JOHN RUSKIN advises, "You must know firmly and feel deeply that you should dedicate every day to the good of your fellows, doing everything you can for them. You have to do it and not talk about it." When doing good, be grateful for the chance to do it, and seek possibilities for doing good at every occasion.
05-22:
The greatest changes in the world are made slowly and gradually, not with eruptions and revolutions. 

"All great thoughts are living thoughts, and they can grow and be changed. And they change and grow as a tree, and not as a cloud." 
—JOHN RUSKIN

"The process of life should be the birth of a soul. This is the highest alchemy, and this justifies our presence on earth." 
—HENRI AMIEL

Nothing hinders inner perfection more than believing you are already doing well, as true moral improvement occurs invisibly over time. If you notice your progress, you may be mistaken, having actually stopped and begun moving backward.
05-23:
The less we need, the less trouble we can have. 

It is a great happiness to have what you desire; but it is an even greater happiness not to want more than you already have. 
—MENEDEMUS

Who is rich?—He who is happy with what he has. 
—The TALMUD

From pleasures appear sadness and fear. He who is free from pleasures does not have either fear or sadness. 
—BUDDHIST WISDOM

The growth of your desires is not the way to perfection... the more a person limits himself, the better he can understand his human dignity, and the more free, the more brave he becomes.
05-24:
Love is one of the manifestations of God in man. The purpose of life is to express love in all its manifestations. To be happy, you should love with self-sacrifice, love all and everything, and spread a network of love everywhere. 

As Paramahansa Yogananda said, "Love is the simplest and greatest thing in the universe."
05-25: Be careful not to follow bad examples, especially when it comes to speaking about others. As it's said, "There is no worse harm for a person with a strong intellect than the temptation to make witty remarks that blame and mock his neighbors." Making jokes at the expense of others is like disguising something distasteful to make it more palatable. It's essential to think before speaking, for "the more you are irritated, the more excited you become, the more care you must exercise not to sin with your words by abusing others."
05-26:
The manner of one’s death can be in accord with the manner of one’s life, and therefore it can be a moral action. As Henri Amiel notes, "an animal simply dies, but a human should return his soul to its creator." 

A good death can have a profound impact, taking away the sins of a bad life, and the words and actions of a dying person have great power over others. In the final moments, a person's life is illuminated, and their words and deeds have special power over those who remain. It is essential to be prepared to die in the best way possible, using the influence of those powerful minutes to leave a lasting impact.
05-27:
The human mind often directs its activity towards hiding the truth, rather than revealing it, which is a major source of temptation. 

A man cannot do everything; but this cannot be an excuse for doing bad things. 
—HENRY DAVID THOREAU

If you see that an action is explained by a very sophisticated reasoning, then you can be sure that this action is bad. The decisions of the conscience are always strict and simple.
05-28:
In a truly Christian world, great wealth is an indication of a man's weakness and lies. 

As Ralph Waldo Emerson noted, people often measure respectability by one's wealth, rather than their inner spiritual value. Jean Jacques Rousseau also observed, "The possession of great wealth is a school of pride, cruelty, self-admiration, and dissipation." Additionally, he stated, "The lack of sensitivity among rich people is not as cruel as their compassion." A rich person should not be proud of his wealth; he should be ashamed of it.
05-29:
Life is the understanding of a holy spirit in yourself; a holy spirit which is put into some limits. 

"The only clear and final truth is the truth of conscience." 
—RENÉ DESCARTES

I know that I could die, but the world will not disappear. Death will make some changes in me, and while I will not be completely destroyed, I will become another being, separate from this world. 

Our life is our limited understanding of this eternal and limitless spirit which knows no constraints in time and space. The human conscience is a divine conscience.
05-30:
Our land, like our character, cannot be the object of buying and selling. 

"Earth is the great gift which nature gave to people, and the birth of every person in this world should give him the right to possess this earth. And this right should be as natural as the right of a person to have the breast of his mother."
—JEAN FRANÇOIS MARMONTEL

"The bodies of men and women, and even more importantly, their children, should not be bought and sold. So too the water, the land and air, because these things are necessary conditions of this existence."
—JOHN RUSKIN

People strive for the possession of many things they can call their property, rather than what is truly good.
05-31:
A person who is not used to luxury often pretends it's natural for them, neglecting it to appear more important. Similarly, a stupid person feigns boredom with life, seeking something more interesting.

The joy of your spirit is the indication of your strength. 
—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

You must believe in the possibility of happiness to be happy. Even without worldly comforts and wealth, one who follows the divine law remains happy. If we're dissatisfied with life, we should be unsatisfied with ourselves, for we don't have the right to be unhappy with our life.
06-01:
Better to do nothing than to do harm. 

We often prioritize obligations over life's simple joys, but these moments are sometimes more important and necessary than other things. Cruel people are busy all the time, as if to justify their cruel dealings.

Pleasure is as important as work and is the reward of work. It's only bad in certain cases: when it exploits others, fosters competition, or is reserved for a select few. Alternating work and pleasure can fill our lives with joy, as long as we avoid these evils.
06-02:
For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted. 

A person who stands on his tiptoes cannot stand long, and a person who is too proud of himself cannot set a good example. 

He who is looking for wisdom is already wise; and he who thinks that he has found wisdom is a stupid man. 

No exterior force can make you humble. There is only one way to be humble: do not think about yourself, but about how you can serve God and others.
06-03:
All creatures are inseparably connected. 

I will never seek or accept merely my own personal salvation. I do not want to receive satisfaction by myself; always and everywhere I live and work, I will hope and strive for the universal salvation of all people and all creatures in the world. 
—ANCIENT CHINESE WISDOM

Understand that you are part of a great spiritual brotherhood; there is something cheering and soothing in the thought which will make you calm and satisfied.
—MARCUS AURELIUS

Humanity has begun to understand that we will all rise or fall together—that we are bound together, as we live together.
—LUCY MALORY
06-04:
Due to the current distortion of Christianity, our lives have become worse than those of pagans. A man should be a servant, and he should make a choice of whose servant he is: if he is the servant of his passions, then he is a servant of other human beings, but if he is the servant of his inner divine spirit, then he is a servant of God alone.

As Ralph Waldo Emerson said, "The salvation of mankind depends upon independent thinkers directing their thoughts rightly." It is also important to question the respect given to different objects, customs, or laws, and to make our religions open, allowing every human being to reveal and discuss religious truth.
06-05:
If we say that the outer world exists only as we see it, we deny that there are other beings, with senses different from our own. 

As Benedictus Spinoza implies, we give everything we see a preexisting form from our imagination. In reality, the outer world in itself is not as we see it, and thus everything material in this world is insignificant. What is important then is the divine spark, the spiritual root of our lives, which exists everywhere, at all times, and for all people.
06-06:
An evil deed not only destroys the soul but often returns to bring evil on the evildoer.

The greatest virtue is to do no evil, even to your enemies. 
—INDIAN WISDOM

When you throw a ball up in the air, it doesn’t stay there but returns to the earth; likewise all your good or bad actions will return to you in another form, 
—BUDDHIST WISDOM

Neither in the sky nor in the earth nor in the depth of mountains, nor anywhere in this world is there a place where one can escape the results of sin.
—DHAMMAPADA, a book of BUDDHIST WISDOM
06-07:
Calmness and humility provide pleasures which are not accessible to the selfish and the proud. The prerequisite of a good life is peace between people, and the major obstacle to peace is pride. 

Come unto me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.
—MATTHEW 11:28-30

Be strict in judging yourself and gentle in judging others, and you will have no enemies.
—CHINESE WISDOM 

Do not be afraid to be humiliated if you can accept this with humility—you will be repaid many times in the spiritual blessings which are connected with being humble.
06-08:
Without truth there is no kindness; without kindness the truth cannot be told. As Luke 6:46-49 reminds us, "And why call ye me, Lord, Lord, and do not the things which I say?" This emphasizes the importance of living our words, rather than just speaking them. 

Lao-Tzu advises, "Always respond to hatred with kindness. The most difficult enterprises are easiest at their inception... Confront difficulties while they are still easy, then, and tackle a big thing when it is still small." Manu also notes, "There are two paths which lead to virtue: the first is to be truthful and just, and the second is to do no evil to living beings." Ultimately, authenticity is key, for "Disguising yourself as a kind person is worse than being nakedly mean."
06-09:
Christian society, as it now exists, doesn’t follow Christian law in its real meaning. 

Almost every effort of the human mind is directed, not toward lightening the work of the laborer, but toward making more pleasant the idleness of the leisured. 

As Saint John Chrysostom notes, "If a person could look at our world from a distance he would see so much stupidity and hatred that he would probably cry." He also observes, "It defies the laws of nature for a child to rule an adult, or a fool to guide a wise man. Likewise, it is against the law of nature that a small group of people should be overindulged when a huge, hungry crowd’s most basic needs aren’t met."

Ultimately, "There is only one thing in this world which is worth dedicating all your life. This is creating more love among people and destroying barriers which exist between them."
06-10:
There is something in the soul which cannot die, which cannot be affected by death. 

"He who defeats others is strong; he who defeats himself is powerful, and he who knows when he dies that he will not be destroyed is eternal." —LAO-TZU

People are born and live only as manifestations of God; therefore they cannot be completely destroyed. Real life exists outside time and space; therefore, death can change the manifestation of life in this world, but it cannot destroy life itself. Try to live with the part of your soul which understands eternity, which is not afraid of death, and that part of your soul is love.
06-11:
All material changes in our everyday life are small in comparison with those in our spiritual life. To change your thoughts and ideas, you should concentrate your conscious mind on your spiritual requirements. 

Every thought a person dwells upon, whether he expresses it or not, either damages or improves his life.
—LUCY MALORY

We are all only the consequences of what we think.
—BUDDHA

We lose good thoughts that could have improved the world and promptly forget about them, without regret, though they are more precious than millions.
06-12:
Suffering is the necessary condition for spiritual and physical growth. 

As John 16:20-21 notes, "You will be sad, and your sadness will become joy. A woman is in pain when she gives birth to a child, and after the birth she doesn’t remember, and she is joyful." 

Ralph Waldo Emerson also observes, "We grow thus: We come closer to God and God comes closer to us as our will becomes united with the will of God." Ultimately, we should look in suffering for the seeds of our future spiritual growth.
06-13:
Intellect is the quality that makes us different from animals. 

Buddha said: “In meditation, in speech, in life, in studies, I never forget about the most important thing: the requirements of the intellect.” 
I cannot came any improvement in anyone except with the help of the goodness and kindness which already is inherent in this person.
—IMMANUEL KANT 

People spoil their life who neglect their intellect and say that it cannot guide them.
06-14:
When you make an effort not to blame other people, your life becomes much easier, but very few people make this small effort. 

A monk who had been rather weak in his life was granted a lovely and honored part of paradise because he had never blamed a single person in his life. 

As ROMANS 2:1 states, "Therefore thou art inexcusable, O man, whosoever thou art that judgest: for wherein thou judgest another, thou condemnest thyself; for thou that judgest doest the same things." 

Additionally, CONFUCIUS notes, "The more strictly and mercilessly you judge yourself, the more just and kind you will be in the judgment of others." The TALMUD also advises, "Do not glorify in blaming and despising other people. A kind person should hide the shame of others, even those who have harmed him." By not blaming others, you will feel the growth of the ability to love in your soul, and see the growth of goodness in your life.
06-15:
To love God means to love the highest possible good which we can imagine in all things. Real love of God is a moral feeling based on a clear understanding of His high, superior being; love of God coincides with love of virtue, truth, and kindness. 

—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

The commandments of God should be followed because of love of God, not because of fear of God. 
—The TALMUD

Only perfection is worth complete love, and we can only achieve this by loving perfection, which is God.
06-16:
The improvement of society can be achieved only by the moral improvement of individuals. 

"We live in an epoch of discipline, culture, and civilization, but not in an epoch of morality." —IMMANUEL KANT

There can be only one way to fight the general evil of life: it is in the moral, religious, and spiritual perfection of your own life.
06-17:
The misfortunes of war and preparations for war bear little relation to the reasons given to explain war. 

As Anatole France notes, "The madness of contemporary war is justified by dynastic interest, common nationalism, European equilibrium, or ambitions." Galston Mohk adds, "You ask me, is it necessary for civilized people to make war? And I tell you not only is it “already” unnecessary, but it was never necessary, and not sometimes but always it destroys the normal development of humanity, destroys justice, and stops progress." The reasons which governments give for wars are always screens, behind which lie completely different reasons and motives.
06-18:
Understanding our duty provides us with the understanding of our divine soul. 

There is in our soul something that, if we see it as it is and give it the proper attention, will always give us great pleasure; this something is the moral disposition or quality which was given to us at our creation.

—IMMANUEL KANT

If your heart is filled with virtue, then you will find happiness and beauty.

—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

The voice of your conscience is the voice of God.
06-19:
Your conscience is your understanding of your spiritual origins; only when you possess such awareness will it be a real guide in your life. There are two different beings within you, one which is blind and sensual, and another which can see and is spiritual. This spiritual being, called conscience, guides us towards goodness.

As GIUSEPPE MAZZINI notes, God gave us the consciousness of mankind as a whole, as well as our own consciousness as individuals, allowing us to come closer to God and understand the truth. JEAN JACQUES ROUSSEAU describes conscience as "the deathless voice of heaven, and a true guide and judge of goodness" that makes a person resemble God. THEODORE PARKER advises, "Always ask yourself, do my actions coincide with my conscience? And do not be afraid to arrive at meanings different from other people’s." Ultimately, we should "Fear all that is not accepted by your conscience."
06-20:
The time will come when people will have the same disgust for the meat of animals as they now have for human flesh. 

As Alphonse Lamartine notes, "Do not raise your hand against your brother, and do not spill the blood of any living creatures who live on this earth, neither human beings nor pets nor wild animals nor birds." Dr. Johann Georg von Zimmermann also observes, "It is terrible to... kill animals and take their dead bodies as your food." In our time, the killing of animals for pleasure or food is almost a crime, and hunting and eating meat are not just trivial things, but bad actions.
06-21:
The misery of the unintellectual life brings us to the need for an intellectual life. I once lived in sin, surrounded by unhappiness and suffering, with no escape in sight except death. But then I heard the words of Christ and understood them, and life transformed from an evil to a source of happiness that surpasses even death.

As Confucius said, "We can understand wisdom in three ways: first, by meditation; this is the most noble way. Secondly, by being influenced by someone or following someone; this is the easiest way. Third is the way of experience; this is the most difficult way." When faced with suffering, it's essential to focus on inner moral and spiritual perfection, rather than escape. Ultimately, suffering brings people closer to their purpose: the appearance of God within themselves and in all mankind.
06-22:
There is only one true religion for all of mankind. 

—IMMANUEL KANT

We can believe in a thing, we can know that it exists, even if we cannot understand it with our intellect, or explain it with words.

If you are a Muslim, go and live as a Christian; if you are a Christian live as a Jew; if you are Catholic, live as an Orthodox—whatever religion you have, hold the same respect for people of different religions. 
—ISLAMIC WISDOM
06-23:
Only he who accepts that the essence or meaning of his life is not material but spiritual can be free. 

A slave who is happy with his state is a slave twice over, because not only his body but his soul is enslaved. 
—EPICTETUS

Peace is the freedom which is based on the acceptance of every person’s rights, and slavery is the negation of rights, and of human dignity.
—After MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO

Remember that you are more free if you change your opinion and follow those who have corrected your mistakes, than if you are stubborn about your mistakes.
—MARCUS AURELIUS

There is only one real knowledge: that which helps us to be free.
—VISHNU PURANA, INDIAN WISDOM
06-24:
Understanding or mindfulness of death teaches one to choose the things which are complete or could be completed; and those things are the most important. 

You could die very quickly; but yet you have time to rid yourself of your passions. Be humble to everyone. 
—MARCUS AURELIUS

Do you worry about the moment when you die? Our life is only a moment in eternity. Think and you will see that you have eternity behind you and before you, and between these two huge abysses, what difference does it make whether you live three days or three centuries?
—MARCUS AURELIUS

Whenever you ask whether you should behave in this way or the other, ask yourself, what would you do if you knew that you could die this evening, and nobody would find out about your action. Death spurs people to finish their affairs; among all actions, there is only one type which is complete, and that is love which seeks no reward.
06-25:
It is very useful to notice the impression which our life and deeds make upon other people. You should live so that other people think well of you and you think well of yourself. 

—LUCY MALORY

The simplest, quickest, and surest means to becoming known as a virtuous person is to work on yourself, to actually be virtuous. 

—SOCRATES

Seek no other support than your inner self and God. 

—DEMOSTHENES
06-26:
Love provides a person with the purpose of his life. Intellect shows him the means to achieve that purpose. 

As Blaise Pascal notes, "a person is a grass which possesses some intellect." This unique quality sets him apart, and as Eastern Wisdom states, "Man differs from other animals only in his intellect." William Ellery Channing also recognizes the importance of intellect, saying, "I praise Christianity because it develops, strengthens, and elevates my intellectual nature."
06-27:
A good life is given only to those who make efforts to achieve it. To attain it, one should not be afraid of any good deeds, having the same strength for small acts as for the greatest good deed. 

As Lao-Tzu says, "In order to have a razor sharp, you should sharpen it. The same should happen with your soul if you are looking for real goodness." Ralph Waldo Emerson also notes, "If there is something great in you, it will not appear on your first call. It will not appear and come to you easily, without any work and effort." 

Ultimately, making an effort to follow God's law and living a virtuous life can lead to a happy and joyful life, as stated in Matthew 7:7-8, "Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you."
06-28:
The family relationship is only good and firm when it goes beyond family and is also religious, with all members believing in one God and His law. 

Family love is a selfish feeling, and therefore it can justify bad, unworthy actions. As Jesus said, "He that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me: and he that loveth son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me." —MATTHEW 10:37

In a higher sense, family love, like self-love, is a natural feeling that can become a vice when it surpasses its limits, but it can never be a virtue. Jesus also said, "My mother and my brethren are these which hear the word of God, and do it." —LUKE 8:20-21
06-29:
Depression is a state of the soul in which you can see no sense either in your own life or in the life of the world. When you have bad spirits or are in a low mood, you should be so in solitude. As one navigates this darkness, it's essential to remember that perceptions can be distorted, much like being drunk. 

The words of wisdom remind us: "O Lord, help me to be always happy and to rejoice following and fulfilling your will." Ultimately, our physical sufferings and periods of depression are part of our life, and we should patiently wait until they are over, or our life is over.
06-30:
As soon as a person asks himself the question, “How do I live my life in the best way?” then all other questions are answered. Real living takes place in the inner domain, where changes can hardly be observed, in our spiritual life. 

And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things: But one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her.

—LUKE 10:41-42

Instead of saving humanity, every person should save himself.

—ALEXANDER HERZEN
07-01:
The human soul is divine. 

Every truth has its origin in God. When it is manifested in a man, this is not because it comes from him, but because he has such a quantity of transparency that he can reveal it.
—BLAISE PASCAL

A person’s purpose on this earth is to be in harmony with eternity. It is only then that the universal flow of love and intellect can be channeled through this person, as if through a clear pathway.
—LUCY MALORY

Keep yourself in purity, far from evil, so that divine power may come through you. And in this flow of divine power through you, there is great bliss.
07-02:
The words of our language are often misused in the criticism of art, especially false art. 

A work of art makes a great impression on us only when it gives us something which, even with all the efforts of our intellect, we cannot understand completely.

—ARTHUR SCHOPENHAUER

A real artist always simplifies, and art has the power to clarify mysteries, make the opaque evident, and the complicated simple.

—After HENRI AMIEL

Remember that you cannot do anything wonderful driven by competition; you cannot do anything noble from pride.

—JOHN RUSKIN

Real art transfers truth from the domain of knowledge to the domain of feelings, and its signs include working for sacrifice rather than profit, and being understandable to all people.
07-03:
A person is enslaved to the extent he believes that his life has only a physical beginning.

Nothing can be done without faith. Hesitation can kill a person, or it can destroy whole nations. 
—F. ROBERT DE LAMENNAIS

If you see a person who is not happy you should know that he is not a free man; he is a slave of something. 
—After EPICTETUS

When you have no freedom, then your life becomes the life of an animal.
—GIUSEPPE MAZZINI

If you feel that you are not free, look for the reason inside you.
07-04:
Punishment is a notion that humankind gradually outgrows.

Every punishment is based, not on logic or on the feeling of justice, but on the desire to wish evil on those who have done evil to you or to another person. As it stands, "To punish others is like putting more wood in the fire. Every crime already has punishment in itself, and it is more cruel and more just than the punishment created by people." 

Our descendants will likely view our current system of punishments as we view cannibalism or human sacrifice, wondering "How did they not see the uselessness and cruelty of those things which they did?" The desire to punish stems from a low animal feeling that should be suppressed, rather than guiding our actions.
07-05:
Solomon and Job knew and spoke wisely about the uselessness of human life. The first was the happiest, the second the unhappiest of all men. 

—BLAISE PASCAL

This is the divine law of life: that only virtue stands firm. All the rest is nothing.

—PYTHAGORAS

If you fear woes and misfortunes, then you are already unhappy. Those who fear misfortunes usually deserve them.

—CHINESE PROVERB

Happiness and calmness are neither inside us nor outside us. They are in God, who is both inside and outside us.

—BLAISE PASCAL
07-06:
Neither the descriptions of war nor its terrible cruelties and atrocities can stop people from participating in it. A witness recounted his experience in the Russian-Japanese war, describing the terrible sight of blood, pieces of flesh, and bodies with heads torn away. 

—HENRI HARDUIN GARDUEN

War is now more terrible than at any period in human history.

—GUY DE MAUPASSANT

The time has come to talk about the evils of war. It is not true that the existence of wars proves their necessity.
07-07:
To deny God is to deny yourself as a spiritual and intellectual being. 

I know God and soul not by their definition, but in another way entirely. As GIUSEPPE MAZZINI says, "God exists. We should not prove this; to deny God is madness." He also notes, "God lives in my conscience, in the conscience of all humanity, in all our universe, and we talk to God in the most important moments of sadness or joy." Ultimately, the existence of God is undeniable, and it is the source of our understanding of ourselves and the world, as evidenced by the fact that "Life in this world goes according to somebody’s will—someone performs special actions upon all life in this world, and touches all our lives."
07-08:
That feeling which solves all of the contradictions of human life and gives one the greatest bliss is known to all people: this feeling is love.

"Pay bad people with your goodness; fight their hatred with your kindness. Even if you do not achieve victory over other people, you will conquer yourself." 
—HENRI AMIEL

"Love destroys death and makes it empty; it gives meaning to senseless things; from unhappiness, love makes real happiness." 
—HENRI AMIEL

If you are in a difficult situation, tell yourself: “I will love everyone whom I meet in this life.” Try to follow this rule; and you will see that everything will find its way, and everything will seem simple, and you will no longer have doubts or fears.
07-09:
People mistakenly think that virtue lies in the knowledge of many things. What is important is not the quantity but the quality of knowledge. 

As Xenophon notes, "Socrates thought that stupidity was incompatible with wisdom, but he never said that ignorance was stupidity." Jean Jacques Rousseau also questions the value of accumulated knowledge, asking, "Have we become wiser for all this? Do we better understand our life, or the meaning of our existence?" 

George Lichtenberg adds, "Reading too much is harmful to your independence of thought. The greatest thinkers I’ve met among scholars are people who do not read too much." Ultimately, it is not the lack of knowledge we should fear, but false knowledge, for "all evil in this world comes from false knowledge."
07-10:
In the world today, real faith has in most cases been replaced by public opinion. 

As Ralph Waldo Emerson said, "God gives a choice to every soul between truth and peace." Additionally, "Faith is not achieved by a majority of votes." The absence of faith is the major reason for the evil that exists today, and people's misfortunes are caused by misunderstanding their duties.
07-11:
Truly virtuous is the person who gives his love to the weak. 

As John Ruskin notes, "Power is given to a person, not to oppress the weak, but to support and to help the weak." Similarly, Mohammed emphasizes that "Every kind thing is a virtue," from simple acts like giving water to a thirsty person to showing a traveler his way. The essence of virtue is also captured in Luke 6:30-31, "Give to every man that asketh of thee; and of him that taketh away thy goods ask them not again. And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise."
07-12:
The foundation of love is in each person’s understanding of the unity of the divine spark which lives in all people. 

As Arthur Schopenhauer notes, every good or charitable action becomes a mysterious and unexplainable thing, because it comes out of the mysterious understanding of the unity of all living beings. We are separate beings, yet in our inner life we are closely connected with all living things. This connection allows us to feel some of the vibrations of this spiritual world, which are moving across the universe, even if they are not yet visible to our eyes.
07-13:
The existing order of things can be improved. 

Wise consumption is much more complicated than wise production. What five people will produce, one person can very easily consume, and the question for each individual and for every nation is not how are we to produce, but how our products are to be consumed.

—JOHN RUSKIN

You can torture people; you can treat them as if they were animals; you can abuse them in all ways; you can kill them as if they were summer flies. But people will remain free in the highest sense because they have eternal souls.

—JOHN RUSKIN
07-14:
God's kingdom is the fulfillment among mankind of God's will, according to the measure in which it is revealed to us. The kingdom of God on earth is the final purpose and desire of humankind. 

As Immanuel Kant said, "The kingdom of God on earth is the final purpose and desire of humankind." Alexandr Arkhangelsky adds, "In religious services we should understand, not the rituals... but love for God and for our neighbor." Ultimately, "The kingdom of God is inside us; therefore look for the kingdom of God inside you and the rest will take care of itself, in the best possible way."
07-15:
My spiritual life is subject neither to suffering nor death. Therefore, salvation from suffering and death lies in the transfer of my conscious “self” from the material to the spiritual.

The way to understand this world is to understand your inner self, based on love and unification with all other creatures. As the Talmud states, "The essence of love of God is in the desire of the soul for its creator, for unity with this higher light." 

Ultimately, surrendering to a higher will is key, as expressed in Luke 22:42, "Yet not My will, but Thine be done." This eternal beginning within us, as noted in The Brahmin's Wisdom, "has always existed, it exists now, and it will always exist: its time will never end."
07-16:
Nothing can support idleness better than empty chatter. A wise person is always wary lest his words surpass his actions.

—CHINESE WISDOM

First think, then speak. Stop when told “enough.” 
—MUSLiH-UD-DIN SAADI

The more people speak, the less desire they have to act.
—THOMAS CARLYLE

The less you speak, the more you will work.
07-17:
The foundation of ancient societies was violence, but our contemporary society is based on wise concord, the negation of violence.

Ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth: But I say unto you, That ye resist not evil: but whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also.

—MATTHEW 5:38-39

He who is really skillful in communicating with people is usually a humble and quiet person. This is called the virtue of nonresistance. This is called harmony with Heaven.

—LAO-TZU

When people use violence, try to convince them not to, using wise and convincing arguments that appeal to their higher spiritual understanding.
07-18:
People believe in eternal life because they believe that life's beginning is spiritual, and therefore eternal.

In our soul is the sign of eternity.
—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

A person is not afraid to die only after he understands that he was never born, that he always existed, exists at present, and will always exist. Those who believe that their life has not begun with their birth and will not end with death find it much easier to live a good life than those who do not understand or believe this.
07-19:
Really true, good, and great things are always simple. 

The language of truth is always simple.
—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

The clearest and simplest notions are almost always concealed by sophisticated meditations.
—MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO

If you want to find an example to follow, you should look among simple, humble folk.
07-20:
The feeling of compassion for other living beings can diminish over time, much like our sensitivity to physical pain. 

The first condition to bringing religion into your life is manifesting love and pity toward all living creatures, as noted in Chinese Wisdom. Compassion for animals is also closely tied to one's character, with Arthur Schopenhauer stating, "You can say with confidence that he who is cruel to animals cannot be a kind man." 

When encountering suffering, rather than fleeing, we should draw closer and try to help, for as Mikhail Goldstein highlights, "Every murder is terrible. But most terrible is the murder in which you want to eat the creature you have killed, when you kill for the satisfaction of your stomach."
07-21:
Love is the manifestation of the divine nature which exists above time. 

As Benedictus Spinoza notes, "The New Testament reflects a very simple faith: faith in God and respect for Him. It is the same in following the law; there is only one law: love your neighbor." 

Love can only exist in the present moment, and it is an action directed toward the goodness of others. Be kind even if the world blames you, for it is better to be kind and blamed than to be praised for being bad.
07-22:
Faith is not true faith if love is not in harmony with it. 

"What doth it profit, my brethren, though a man say he hath faith, and have not works? Can faith save him?" —JAMES 2:14. This emphasizes that faith without action is empty. As Eastern wisdom notes, "A person who knows the law but does not fulfill it reminds me of one who plows the land but does not put seed in it." Ultimately, true faith requires both belief and works to be justified.
07-23:
Effort is the necessary condition of moral perfection. 

The body is the first student of the soul. 
—HENRY DAVID THOREAU

Nothing more can be considered as real merit for a person than his effort. Only in his effort is a person shown in his real light.
—The KORAN 

If you were abused, make an effort to love those who have abused you. If you have abused someone, make an effort to correct the evil which you created.
07-24:
When one understands life's law, one manifests the part of God which lives inside him. 

As Confucius said, "Every man, from the king to the poorest pauper, should seek his own perfection, because only self-perfection improves mankind." 

In the long run, people achieve only that which they have set as goals for themselves; therefore, set the highest possible goals for yourself. The fulfillment of the law of goodness can lead to the highest elevation of spirit, often through sufferings that clash with material prosperity.
07-25:
Sometimes we cannot see any connection between our suffering and our sin, but this connection definitely exists. 
As Marcus Tullius Cicero said, "The reward for virtue is the understanding of the good deed." Look for the causes of the evil from which you suffer in yourself, for the source is always in you, and salvation from it lies in changes in your actions, your way of life.
07-26:
In every faith, only what is spiritual is true. 
"God is a spirit, and those who worship Him must worship in spirit and truth." 
—JOHN
If you do not believe that at its beginning, your life came from spirit, why are you looking for it elsewhere? 
Do not be afraid to get rid of things which distract your attention, everything material, to purify the spiritual core of your faith.
07-27:
Knowledge is a tool, not a purpose. 

How to live your life is the only real knowledge. 

People who think that the most important thing in life is knowledge remind me of a butterfly who flies into the flame of a candle, and in so doing burns, and extinguishes the light. 
—GEORGE LICHTENBERG

The purpose of life is the fulfillment of the law of God, not the acquisition of ever more knowledge.
07-28:
Repentance always precedes perfection. It is sad that people think repentance is unnecessary. 

As Frederick William Robertson notes, "It is dangerous to see the cause of evil as lying outside ourselves; for them repentance becomes impossible." This sentiment is echoed by Otto von Bismarck, who reflects on his past with regret, saying, "I have a burden on my soul... I was the cause of the beginning of three big wars. About 800,000 people were killed because of me on the battlefields, and their mothers, brothers, and widows cried for them. And now this stands between me and God." Ultimately, acknowledging and accepting our mistakes is crucial, for "Not to accept your mistakes is to increase them."
07-29:
The more important something is in your life, the more harmful it can be for you in the future if you abuse it. When the intellect becomes the slave of vices and passions, it becomes not only a perversion, but an illness, and we cannot see the difference between truth and falsehood, good and evil, virtue and vice.

—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

Our intellect can only answer the question: “How shall I live?” And the answer is simple: “We should live so as to bring good to all people.” The purpose of the intellect is to reveal the truth; therefore it is a harmful misconception to pervert the truth with intellectual efforts.
07-30:
Only those who know their weaknesses can be tolerant of the weaknesses of their neighbors. 

As the Talmud advises, "My children, if someone abuses you with words, do not pay much attention; be quiet at your prayer, and ask your friends to settle the dispute and find peace between you and those who have abused you." 

Edward Herbert notes, "The man who cannot forgive destroys a bridge which he will have to cross, because every person needs forgiveness." 

Muslih-ud-Din Saadi also reminds us, "A deep river is not troubled if you throw a stone into it. If a religious person is hurt by criticism, then he is not a river but a shallow pool. Forgive others, and then you will receive forgiveness." This wisdom encourages us to look within, acknowledge our own flaws, and cultivate empathy and understanding for others.
07-31: If Christians would follow the law, there would be no rich and no poor. Jesus said, "If thou wilt be perfect, go and sell that thou hast, and give to the poor, and thou shalt have treasure in heaven: and come and follow me." —MATTHEW 19:16, 21. Wealth can be likened to manure, making a bad smell when piled up, but fertilizing the soil when distributed. As the Talmud notes, "A rich person can be insensitive and completely indifferent to the woes of others."
08-01:
The intellect can liberate a person; the less of an intellectual life one has, the less liberated one is.

"A wise man becomes free when he is ready to die at any moment." 
—Diogenes

Freedom cannot be achieved by looking for it, but in looking for truth. Freedom should not be a purpose, but a consequence. As Epictetus noted, a truly free person recognizes their own freedom, and cannot be enslaved, for they "prefer to die as captives" rather than serve.
08-02:
If you are a spiritual being, then the body limits your spiritual being, and death is only a change. Our body gives form to our spiritual being, like a vessel holding a liquid or gas. If the vessel is broken, its contents flow out and may take on a new form or unite with other beings. 

—THEODORE PARKER said, "Death is one step in a continuous development." 
—MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO noted, "Our last day does not bring destruction, only a change." 
For those who live a spiritual life, there is no death, as death is a liberation of the soul from the material personality.
08-03:
We expect rewards for goodness and punishments for wrongdoing, but they may not be immediately forthcoming. As the Talmud notes, "The reward for virtue is virtue, and the punishment for vice is more vice." The worst punishment is the understanding that you failed to properly use the good things given to you, a remorse that can be a hard punishment. 

As Jean Jacques Rousseau says, "You seek the cause of evil, and it is only in yourself." Doing good is the path to bliss, and your actions are their own reward, while the punishment for evil acts lies in your soul.
08-04:
Self-abnegation is not the rejection of your entire self, just the rejection of your animal being. 

"Every person has within himself the capability to understand the life of all humanity." —EDWARD CARPENTER

"Real life starts with self-sacrifice." —THOMAS CARLYLE

The more a person gives to other people, and the less he asks for himself, the better for him. Deny your personality to understand the highest good in life: love, which brings the greatest bliss through self-sacrifice and freedom.
08-05:
We are too likely to accept the views and thoughts of other people without trying to investigate them deeper and further ourselves. 

"Your thought is easily influenced by the presence of another soul; a person can be completely free only when he is alone." 
"Nothing is more harmful than a bad example set by others."
—MARCUS AURELIUS

False ideas often gain currency with loud fanfare, whereas real truth does not need outer embellishments. A person with high morals must be strict about their words and actions, as they can influence others.
08-06:
The intellect is the only fit guide for human life. 

As Luke 11:34-35 states, "The light of the body is the eye: therefore when thine eye is single, thy whole body also is full of light; but when thine eye is evil, thy body also is full of darkness." A person who lives an intellectual life carries this light within, never coming to a dark place, for their intellect lights the way to the end. This distinction is crucial, as some people live and act according to their own thoughts, while others follow the thoughts of others.
08-07:
Vanity has no limits. A person filled with vanity is so involved with himself that there is no room inside him for anyone or anything else. 

As Jean De La Bruyère notes, "You could choose no worse way to guide your life than to follow the opinions of other people." Additionally, he observes, "When we praise other people it is because we think that they look like us; often to respect a person means to put him on the same level as oneself." Johann Wolfgang Von Goethe also warns, "Real virtue never looks back on its shadow, fame." It's a reminder that true worth shouldn't be measured by external validation.
08-08:
The opinion of a revered writer or thinker can have a deep influence on society; it can also be a big obstacle to understanding real truth. 

As Henry David Thoreau notes, "the first step to moral perfection is your liberation from the religion in which you were raised." Divine truth may be revealed in unexpected places, such as "the speech of children, or in the ravings and nightmares of madmen; or in the ordinary conversations of simple people." 

A thought's validity is not determined by its source, as "a thought which is expressed in the Old Testament, or in the New Testament, or in the Koran or in the Indian divine book the Upanishads, does not become a truth because it is expressed in a book which is considered to be holy."
08-09:
The majority of evils people commit are the result, not of an evil will, but of the dissemination of evil thoughts which people accept and follow uncritically.

—HENRI AMIEL notes, "Material results are the manifestations of unseen forces... all important events originate in a thought." 

"LUC DE VAUVENARGUES" also states, "Our actions are neither as good nor as vicious as our desires." 

Guiding your thoughts is one of the keys to self-perfection. If you suffer misfortunes in your life, look for their cause, not in your actions, but in the thoughts which inspired them, and try to improve those thoughts.
08-10:
You make your decisions in the present, and the present exists out of time; it is a tiny moment where two periods—the past and the future—meet. In the present you are always free to make your choice.

A wise man said, “The most important time is the present time, because at this time a person has power over himself.” 

—HARRIET MARTINEAU said, “Only when we use every minute of our life will we know that we are eternal.” 

The present is the moment in which the divine nature of life is revealed. Let us respect our present time; God exists in this time.
08-11:
A person dies as he lives his spiritual life, alone. 

You must believe that eternal goodness exists within you, and that it grows and develops as long as you live. 
—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

Only your inner self can damn you, and only your inner self can save you.
—DHAMMAPADA, a book of BUDDHIST WISDOM

Every person has a depth to his inner life, an essence that cannot be explained. Hence the need for your own channel of communication with God. Establish this channel and do not seek anything else.
08-12:
The burden, the cross, which every man has to carry exists on two planes: a longer vertical plane, which is the will of God; and a much shorter, horizontal plane, which is a man’s own will. Real happiness can be built only by harmonizing your life with the will of God.

"He who is not with Me is against Me; and he who does not gather with Me, scatters." 
—LUKE 11:23

If you want to fulfill the will of God, all will be good; if you want to fulfill your own will, and it does not coincide with the will of God, all will be evil. The only way to know God and to believe in eternity is to unite your own will with the will of God.
08-13:
To be wise, you must follow your intellect, even though such a way of life is often attacked. 

As the TALMUD notes, "The heavens do not approve when we sin, and the earth does not approve when we are virtuous." 

LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA advises, "Do not be interested in the quantity of people who respect and admire you, but in their quality. If bad people dislike you, so much the better." 

EASTERN WISDOM reminds us, "Human intellect is a divine lamp, and its light penetrates to the depth of things." Wisdom reveals the stupidity of a bad life, and people will naturally criticize it, for they cannot endure the revelation of their own flaws without resistance.
08-14:
People have become too accustomed to the use of force. Life without violence seems impossible for them to imagine. Two ways exist to guide human activity: forcing someone to act against their wishes or guiding their wishes through reasoning. 

As Abraham Comb notes, "One is the way of violence: it is used by ignorant people, and it leads to complete disappointment. The other is supported by experience, and is always successful." Giuseppe Mazzini also observes, "Without faith in God, you can force people to act, but you cannot convince them. You can become a tyrant, but not a teacher." Ultimately, people will evolve from a state of violence to one of harmony and understanding, but every act of violence delays this transition.
08-15:
All other arguments against meat eating are meaningless in relation to the fact that animals have the same life spirit that exists in us. We should feel that by taking an animal life we commit something close to suicide.

"Eating a lot of meat can make your body stronger, but it weakens your mind." 
—PLUTARCH

"It is very important not to kill children’s natural taste by forcing them to become meat eaters, not only for their health, but for the sake of their character. We do not know the reason, but we know that people who eat a lot of meat are usually cruel." 
—JEAN JACQUES ROUSSEAU

The more compassion a person shows other living creatures, the kinder and better he is. To kill animals, whether for sport or for fuel, is very cruel.
08-16:
We are spiritually connected on all sides, not only with people but with all living creatures. As Arthur Schopenhauer notes, we possess two ways of understanding the world: one of division and one of universal unification, where "all people seem very close to us, and all are equal among themselves" and compassion and love are aroused. 

Giuseppe Mazzini emphasizes our shared origin and purpose, stating, "All people have the same origin, are bound by the same law, and were created for the same purpose." Marcus Aurelius advises, "We should always try to find those things which do not separate us from other people but which unite us." By embracing this unity, we can serve and suffer with all living beings, recognizing our deep connection to all of existence.
08-17:
Kindness is a necessary addition to everything. All the best qualities of mankind are meaningless and worthless without kindness, and even the worst vices can be easily forgiven with kindness. 

There is a natural kindness that comes from external attributes, and another type that comes from inner, spiritual work - the latter being less attractive but constant and ever-growing. 

As Confucius said, "The goodness which you do gives you pleasure, but not satisfaction. No matter how much goodness you do, you should wish to do more and more." Ultimately, kindness is the major quality of the soul, and its absence is often a result of being subjected to lies, passions, or temptations that violate one's natural state.
08-18:
Christianity is true because it can answer the most distant, abstract questions, and at the same time, it can solve all practical issues of life as well. 

—F. ROBERT DE LAMENNAIS said, "Christ is still on his cross, waiting for his apostles... because the suffering is terrible, and his eyes are tired of looking to the east and waiting for the sunrise of the time of the lord."

"Religion is true not because it was taught by the saints, but the saints taught it because it is true." —GOTTHOLD EPHRAIM LESSING

Only religion destroys egoism and selfishness, so that one starts to live life not only for himself, and sets a person free from outer pressures.
08-19:
Life is constant movement, and therefore goodness in life is not a certain state, but the direction of movement. This direction is not in serving yourself, but God.

As Blaise Pascal notes, "Those people who are really close to goodness understand that real goodness for one is when all people have this goodness and share it among themselves." 

Happiness can be achieved by struggle, but goodness is achieved by being humble. Real goodness is to serve God, and it should be done without choosing to whom, for good things, once done, will never disappear.
08-20:
Anyone who is engaged in really important things is very simple because he does not have time to create unnecessary things.

As Henri Amiel notes, "Every desire abates, and every vice grows after it is satisfied." Voltaire also observes, "Every new desire is the beginning of a new wish, the beginning of a new sadness." Socrates adds, "to wish nothing is the happiness of God, and when you wish to have only small things, then you make yourself closer to this divine and high happiness." Ultimately, a life of moderation is key, where one can live without need and jealousy, and find happiness in simplicity.
08-21:
A fruitful prayer is the establishment in your conscious mind of the understanding of life’s meaning. 

The desire of our heart to please God with all our actions—this is the spirit of real prayer, as Immanuel Kant notes. 

But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking. Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him. 
—MATTHEW 6:7-9

Real prayer is about remembering your obligations to God amidst life's distractions, and with time, this habit can be cultivated, making every moment a chance to connect with a higher force.
08-22:
It is dangerous to disseminate the idea that our life is purely the product of material forces and that it depends entirely on these forces. 

As Lucy Malory notes, "The wiser a person is, the simpler the language he uses to express his thoughts." This simplicity is often lacking in false science and false religion, which "express their dogmas in highly elevated language to make simple people think that they are mysterious, important, and attractive."

The real purpose of science is the understanding of the truth of this life, not the justification of evil. The teaching about your inner life is the most useful one, and it's essential to distinguish between true understanding and the "judicial sciences, political sciences, and especially theology, the science of religion" that can be used to justify evil.
08-23:
If people were completely virtuous, they would never step aside from the truth. 

And this is the condemnation, that light is come into the world, and men loved darkness rather than light, because their deeds were evil. 

—JOHN 3:19-21

Don’t be afraid of a person in any position... If you respect all people, you should love all people, and fear no one.

—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

Truth can be understood only by waiting and watching, and when you get one truth, two more will appear before you.

—JOHN RUSKIN
08-24:
People move, inconspicuously but ceaselessly, toward the kingdom of God, which will be achieved by unification in love. 

As F. Robert de Lamennais notes, "Every single individual person, as well as all mankind together, will change, and will go on to higher stages without stopping in their development, the limit of which is God himself." Giuseppe Mazzini also reminds us, "No, the word of all-powerful God has not been told completely. And his thought has not been understood completely in all its depth." 

The purpose of life is loving, the penetration of everything with love, a slow and gradual change from evil to good, as embodied in the words of Luke 12:49, 51, "I am come to send fire on the earth; and what will I, if it be already kindled? Suppose ye that I am come to give peace on earth? I tell you, Nay; but rather division." The human world is always on the way to perfection, with the understanding of this process being one of mankind's biggest joys.
08-25:
Work is the condition of existence in the material, physical world. If any would not work, neither should he eat. —II THESSALONIANS 3:10

Nature does not stop in its development, and it executes all kinds of idleness. —JOHANN WOLFGANG VON GOETHE

You should never be ashamed of any work, even the lowest and dirtiest, but you should be ashamed only of the dirtiest moral state, that is the idleness of your body, which is the necessary result of the consumption of the labor of others.
08-26: Justice is achieved not in striving for justice, but with love. As Marcus Aurelius notes, "You cannot be completely just," and instead, one must "always change things, to improve things, to make them better." To achieve this, Aurelius advises, "in order to be just, you should make a self-sacrifice, be unjust to yourself." Ultimately, the key is to "remain humble" despite encountering injustice, and to continually strive for improvement.
08-27:
The best and most important object for every person is his inner self, his spiritual being. A person who knows all sciences but does not know himself is a poor and ignorant person, whereas he who does not know anything except for his inner spiritual self is an enlightened person.

As Henry David Thoreau advises, "When you feel the desire for power, you should stay in solitude for some time." A Chinese Proverb also notes, "The way to virtue goes through the deserts." Ultimately, a person always has a place to be safe from all of his misfortunes, and this place is his soul, for "if you could only know who you are, all your troubles would seem utterly unnecessary and trivial."
08-28:
Faith is the foundation on which all else rests; it is the root of all knowledge. There are two types of faith: faith in people and faith in God, with the latter being unvarying and necessary for all people. 

As the teachings of Christ guide us, "Cleave to the teachings of Christ completely, and let go of other teachings." Ultimately, faith is the necessary property of the soul, and people who believe they do not have faith are wrong; they simply don’t realize it, or don’t want it, or cannot express it.
08-29:
If, in his soul, a person understands God, he understands his connection to all the world’s people.

"Do not be proud, no matter what high position you occupy in life. In you and in me and in every other person lives the same God, the same life force; you look down on me in vain; we are all equal beings." —INDIAN WISDOM

"I am filled with a great thought: that of the greatness of my soul, its unity with God." —HENRI AMIEL
08-30:
Those who achieve spiritual heights can see the heavenly sunrise sooner than those who live a material life, but the time will come when everyone will see it. 

As Edward Brown said, "We should hope that the day will come when all people perceive that the life of a man is great only in its service to his brothers, for he is united with them by God." William Ellery Channing also noted, "Powerful forces are at work in this world... a new understanding of religion, a new respect for people, a new feeling of brotherhood." Ultimately, when the higher ideal is put before us, all false ideals will fade away.
08-31:
The creation and sale of most art today is pure prostitution, driven by profit rather than an urgent need for expression. 

As John Ruskin notes, "You cannot sell your talent, your genius; as soon as you do, you are a prostitute. You can sell your work, but not your soul." Real art comes from a deep-seated need to express inner feelings, while false art is crafted for the market, corrupting and weakening our spiritual power. Until the pursuit of profit is removed from the artistic process, true art will be overshadowed by the proliferation of false, corrupting influences.
09-01:
People who want to save themselves from life's challenges often distract themselves with various activities, such as vanity, cards, or substance abuse, to avoid confronting the reality of their existence. 

As Leo Tolstoy once said, "Making yourself addicted is not a crime, but it is a preparation for crime." If indulging in addictive behaviors is truly of no importance, then why not stop, especially when it harms oneself and sets a bad example for others?
09-02:
The closer people are to the truth, the more tolerant they are of the mistakes of others. 

As William Warburton notes, "if a good end can be achieved only through bad means, either it is not good after all, or its time has not yet come." Jean France Duclos also observes that "Unbelievers can be equally intolerant as those who believe with crude, primitive understanding." A real truth, a real faith, needs neither worldly support nor an outer glamour, nor does it need to be forcefully introduced to others, for as is said, "God has time; for Him thousands of years pass as one."
09-03:
God cannot be understood by the human intellect, only felt by the human heart. As Philemon notes, "Believe in God, serve Him, but don’t try to understand His essence." We can only know that He exists, and our understanding is limited. 

Omar Khayyam adds, "No one except God comprehends the secret of the great beginning; no one can step outside himself." Our dependence on God can be felt, much like an infant feels the care of its mother, without needing to comprehend the details. Ultimately, the notion of God is not about clear expression, but about the depth of feeling and faith.
09-04:
Real goodness is not something that can be acquired in an instant, but only through constant effort, because real goodness lies in constantly striving for perfection. 

"Renew yourself completely every day, and starting afresh, from the beginning." 
—CHINESE WISDOM

"The journey of the wise to virtue is as a journey to a remote land, or the ascent of a high mountain. People who travel to a faraway place start with a single step, and those who climb a high mountain start from the bottom." 
—CONFUCIUS

Strive for goodness without any expectations for rapid or noticeable success, for the effort of striving for goodness, the process itself, justifies our lives.
09-05:
You should teach others with a good example, but if you teach with evil, then you do not teach, but destroy. Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times: but, Until seventy times seven. —MATTHEW 18:21-22

If you think someone is guilty of wronging you, forgive him. As Thomas Jefferson noted, "The American Indians had no laws, no punishments, and no government. They obeyed the moral understanding of good and evil that is part of every human nature." Punishment is always cruel, always painful, and the existence of a science of punishment is "one of the most ignorant and offensive types of action known to man."
09-06:
People jump back and forth in pursuit of pleasures only because they see the emptiness of their lives more clearly than they do the emptiness of whichever new entertainment attracts them.

—BLAISE PASCAL

We try to make our life more comfortable, but in doing so, we often destroy our present. As it's said, "A misconception remains a misconception, even when it is shared by the majority of the people." Many foolishly believe the world's senselessness and cruelty happens outside their own lives, but it's an illusion that interferes with their way of life.
09-07:
If life is good, then death, a necessary part of life, is good as well. Real life exists only in the present, and its purpose is to fulfill the eternal law of God. 

As John Ruskin said, "Do not bother about what will happen someday, somewhere, in the far away distance, in a future time; think and be very attentive to what happens now, here, in this place." 

When we focus on the present, we become the architect of our life, and going into the future or past leads us away from God, causing feelings of loneliness and enslavement.
09-08:
At that time Jesus answered and said, I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes. 

The world would be a terrible place without newborn children, who bring with them innocence, and the hope of man’s further perfection. —JOHN RUSKIN

Childhood is blessed by heaven because it brings a piece of paradise into the cruelties of life. —HENRI AMIEL

You should respect every person, but you should respect a child above all, and not destroy the innocent purity of his soul.
09-09:
The knowledge we now accept as science interferes with more than it supports the goodness of human life. 

As John Ruskin notes, "Science fulfills its purpose, not when it explains the reasons for the dark spots on the sun, but when it understands and explains the laws of our own life, and the consequences of violating these laws." Ultimately, no matter how great our knowledge may be, it cannot help us fulfill our life’s major purpose—our moral perfection.
09-10:
The voice of conscience makes no mistakes, wanting not the fulfillment of the animal self, but its denial or sacrifice. 

As our self-interest diminishes, our anxieties disappear, and then comes quiet and firm joy, which always diffuses us with a good spiritual disposition and a clear conscience.
—ARTHUR SCHOPENHAUER

A person who lives only for the concerns of his body may become lost in the labyrinth of an animal and sensual life, while the soul will always find truth; it will find the way.
—LUCY MALORY

The voice of your conscience can always be singled out above the noise of your other wishes, because it always wants something seemingly useless, but at the same time something actually beautiful and good, which can be achieved only through effort.
09-11:
The real faith attracts people to itself not because it promises goodness to those who believe, but because it is the only escape, not only from all the problems and troubles and misfortunes of our life, but also from the fear of death. 

As it's been said, "It is bad not to have something for which you are ready to die." This highlights the importance of having a strong foundation of faith. 

Ultimately, "Salvation lies not in rituals and not in a particular creed, but in a clear understanding of the meaning of your life," emphasizing that true faith is rooted in a deeper understanding of one's purpose.
09-12:
You cannot serve both god and the devil. Merely tending to the growth of your wealth has nothing to do with the requirements of the truly spiritual life. 

The love of great wealth commends you, “Bring me your soul as a sacrifice,” and people will do so. 
—SAINT JOHN CHRYSOSTOM

Excessive wealth interferes with the movements of our soul. 
—DEMOSTHENES

The desire for wealth can never be satisfied. 
—MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO

Do not fear poverty, but beware wealth. If only people who seek wealth could clearly see what they would lose by having it, they would put the same amount of effort into getting rid of this wealth as they now put into acquiring it.
09-13:
A wise man does not wish to change his situation, because he knows that it is possible to fulfill the law of God, the law of love, in every situation.

"I never complain about my fate. Once, I did not have shoes, and I complained to God. I went into church with a heavy heart and in the church I saw a man without both feet. So I thanked God that he had given me both feet, and that my only problem was that they were unshod." —MUSLIH-UD-DIN SAADI

"A wise man innately knows how to act without searching, because he has the divine within himself." —LAO-TZU

Try to establish an inner silence in yourself, a complete silence of your lips and your heart, to hear how God speaks to us and know how to fulfill His will. The more upset a person is with others and circumstances, and the more satisfied he is with himself, the further he is from wisdom.
09-14:
Violence is harmful because it is usually dressed in wealth, and it therefore arouses some respect for those things which should arouse disgust. People who have power often rely on violence to maintain the existing order, but this order is actually based on public opinion. 

As Victor Consideránt notes, "A person is not created either to subdue others, or to follow the orders of others. People are corrupted by both ways of behavior." Henry David Thoreau adds, "Our life would become wonderful if we could see all the disgusting things which exist in it." Ultimately, "Subduing people through violence is never justice," as Blaise Pascal observes. Buddhist Wisdom warns, "He who depends on violence behaves wrongly... only he who is free from hatred and fear is truly wise."
09-15:
The biggest obstacles to understanding the truth are lies disguised as truth. In the domain of thoughts and the intellect, misconceptions may be accepted as truth for thousands of years, making a laughingstock of whole nations and enslaving people. 

As Arthur Schopenhauer notes, "The exposure of a lie is as valuable to a community as a clearly expressed truth." He also observes, "Freeing a person from misconceptions, false truths, and lies does not take anything from him; it gives him something important." The progress of mankind lies in revealing these hidden obstacles, and once the truth is victorious, it can never be taken away.
09-16:
Doubts do not destroy truth; they strengthen it. 

As Harriet Martineau notes, "Disbelief is not when a person believes or does not believe in something. It is when he prophesies those things in which he does not believe." 

In moments of self-delusion, a person may forget that their physical life cannot interfere with their spiritual life. Henry David Thoreau wisely observes, "A wise man has doubts even in his best moments. Real truth is always accompanied by hesitations. If I could not hesitate, I could not believe." 

He who hesitates is not distanced from God; it is he who believes unhesitatingly in someone else’s word that God exists or does not exist who is far from God.
09-17:
The individual ownership of large areas of land is as unjust as the ownership of other people. 

As Herbert Spencer notes, "You cannot say that the existing laws regarding the possession of land are lawful. Violence, crime, and power have their source in these laws." Henry George also observes, "Private ownership of land came to be, not out of any natural relationships between people, but through robbery." This highlights the inherent injustice of land ownership, which is linked to many other injustices that protect it.
09-18:
Life’s essence lies, not in your body, but in your conscience. The divine spark lives in all of us, and perpetually strives toward its origin. —LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

You cannot see the soul, but only the soul can truly see the essence of things. —The TALMUD

I call spirit that part of man which has independent existence and gives us the understanding of life. —MARCUS AURELIUS

Let your spiritual side guide your material side, and not the other way around, striving for spiritual not physical perfection.
09-19:
The understanding that we should serve God can enlighten people's lives and ease their burdens. 

As Marcus Aurelius said, "Life is short. Do not forget about the most important things in our life, living for other people and doing good for them." We should also heed the words of Matthew 20:28, "The Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life as a ransom for many." By accepting ourselves as servants, we can find calmness and peace, and be filled with a clear vision of our purpose and great joy.
09-20: All good things can be achieved only with effort. As the Dhammapada notes, "Bad things are easy to do, good things are done only with work and effort." This idea is echoed in Matthew 7:13-14, "Enter by the narrow gate; for the gate is wide, and the way is broad that leads to destruction, and many are those who enter by it. For the gate is small, and the way is narrow that leads to life, and few are those who find it." John Ruskin also emphasizes the need for perseverance, saying, "The way to true knowledge does not go through soft grass covered with flowers. To find it, a person must climb steep mountains." Ultimately, the pursuit of truth and spiritual growth requires dedication and hard work, as Blaise Pascal suggests, "Look for the truth; it wants to be found."
09-21:
The most important and necessary expression of freedom is to give your thought a specific direction. 

Work toward the purification of your thoughts, for without bad thoughts you will be incapable of bad deeds, as Confucius advises. 

We cannot prevent bad thoughts from appearing, but we can choose whether to let them take hold, as Martin Luther notes: "We cannot prevent birds from flying over our heads, but we can keep them from making nests on top of our heads." 

Ultimately, it is a sin not only to do bad things, but even to think about them, as Zoroaster cautions.
09-22:
Faith in the existence of eternity is our exclusively human quality. 

The soul does not live in the body as in a house, but as in a tent, a place of temporary dwelling. 
—INDIAN WISDOM

Mortal people cannot live long; we have only a few moments. But our soul does not age. It believes in eternal things, and it will live for all eternity. 
—TORICLIDIS

Our understanding of eternity is the voice of God who lives within us.
09-23:
No matter how big mankind’s store of knowledge seems, it is only an infinitely small part of all possible knowledge. Socrates noted the futility of seeking to know everything, saying, "Is it true that people are so concerned with these earthly things? People wrongly think that they should know everything."

As John Ruskin observed, "Knowledge is limitless, and the most scholarly and educated person is as far from true knowledge as an uneducated peasant." Additionally, Immanuel Kant noted, "We cannot imagine the scope of our ignorance, just as a blind man cannot imagine darkness until he can see." It is better to know less than necessary than to know more than necessary, and to fear unnecessary knowledge that serves only vanity.
09-24:
It would be possible to eat meat if it were justified by any serious considerations. But it isn’t; and meat eating is simply a bad thing that exists without any justification at all.

—PLUTARCH 
"What nature of struggle for existence or kind of madness forces you to shed blood with your hands in order to eat animals?" 

If we have all the comforts of life, including an abundance of plant-based foods, then eating meat is a choice that lacks justification, making it a sin for the educated and informed to continue this behavior.
09-25:
Work is not a virtue, but it is the necessary condition of a virtuous life. 

—HENRY DAVID THOREAU

Real work is always quiet, constant, and inconspicuous. It is not enough to be a hardworking person, think: what do you work at? For every idle person, there is another person who works too much. Much of the activity of idle people who pretend that they are busy with work is merely entertaining; it just adds a burden to other people.
09-26: All true wisdom and all true faith are clearly expressed in the same moral law. As Marcus Aurelius notes, "All the world is subject to one law, and all thinking beings have the same basic intellect. Therefore, all wise men share the same idea of perfection." Immanuel Kant also reflects on the importance of moral law, stating, "The more I dedicate my time to two things, the more they fill my life with ever-increasing pleasure. The first is the sky above me, and the second is the moral law within me." This universal principle is further emphasized in the words of Matthew 7:12, "Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets."
09-27:
Blaming others is an entertainment that causes harm, and it's a sin not to stop people from practicing it. 

If you want to blame me, you should not be with me. You should be inside me.
—ADAM MICKIEWICZ

Our greatest imperfections are in our inner vision. 
—EDWARD BROWN

As soon as you start blaming a person, stop yourself.
09-28:
Most people act, not according to their meditations, and not according to their feelings, but as if hypnotized, based on some senseless repetition of patterns. 

"You should be brave enough to use your own intellect, in life and in your education." —IMMANUEL KANT

If you see that you are not behaving according to your inner desires, but because of some outer influence, stop and consider whether what drives you is good or bad.
09-29: Worse than all the troubles and horrors of war is the perversion of minds it causes.
