As Blaise Pascal notes, "Is there anything more absurd than a person having a right to kill me because we live on two opposite banks of the river, and our kings quarrel with each other?" The futility of war will eventually become clear, as Charles Riche suggests, "The time will come when people will understand the stupidities of war." It's essential not to try to justify war or the existence of the military, as this can poison the heart and prevent true understanding.
09-30:
The more lonely a person is, the more clearly he can hear the voice of God.

"In the important questions of life, we are always alone. Our deepest inner thoughts cannot be understood by others." —HENRI AMIEL

Temporary solitude from all things in this life, the meditation within yourself about the divine, is food as necessary for your soul as material food is for your body.
10-01:
A sage is not afraid of lack of knowledge: he is not afraid of hesitations, or hard work, but he is afraid of only one thing—to pretend to know the things which he does not know.

—MICHEL DE MONTAIGNE

Never be ashamed to admit what you do not know.

—ARABIC PROVERB

Real wisdom comes from knowing which is the better thing and which is the worse, and therefore, what should be done and what should be done later. To be wise, one must study both good and bad thoughts and acts, but one should study the bad first, to know what is not clever, just, or necessary to do.
10-02:
Religion is that which tells a person who he is and what the nature of the world in which he lives is. 

We should teach our children those principles that are common to all religions, for the moral science of love and the unification of all people. As it stands, "Most people do not listen to God but adore him. It is better not to adore but to listen." Ultimately, "Moral teaching is not complete if it is not religious, but religious teaching is useless if it is not based on morality, that is, if it does not lead to a good life."
10-03:
Big wealth will not give you satisfaction. The more your wealth grows, the more your requirements grow with it. 

As Arthur Schopenhauer notes, "It is difficult if not impossible to find some reasonable limit for acquiring more and more property." A more lasting approach is to acquire the kind of wealth that truly endures, as an Indian Proverb suggests: "You should acquire the kind of wealth which cannot be stolen from you by thieves, which people in power cannot take from you, which will stay with you even after your death, never diminishing and never disappearing. This wealth is your soul." Ultimately, limiting our requirements is always within our power, offering a path to freedom from the cycle of desire and dissatisfaction.
10-04:
Real love refers not just to love for a particular person but to the spiritual state of loving everyone. To love means to live within the lives of those whom you love. 

As Lao-Tzu said, a holy person is concerned most of all about his attitude to people, and can feel all people. 

Without love, nothing can bring goodness, and every action inspired with love will bring fruits afterward, as noted in the Book of Divine Thoughts. Theodore Parker also observed that "Religion is the highest form of love." Ultimately, the more a person expresses his love, the more people love him, and the easier it is for him to love others, making love eternal.
10-05:
Your spirit must constantly assert itself because your body is constantly exerting itself. 

As Confucius said, "What is not easy to do should be done with great persistence." Blaise Pascal also noted, "We suffer from our vices, and try to struggle with them, and the reason for this struggle is that we are not perfect. But our salvation is in this struggle with vices..." A good thing is always done with an effort, and when the effort is repeated, it becomes a habit.
10-06: Illness should be viewed as a natural condition of life. To find balance, take care of your body only to the extent that it helps you serve others. As Marcus Tullius Cicero notes, "Illnesses of the mind are much more dangerous than illnesses of the body." Do not be afraid of illness, and remember that being ill does not free you from your moral requirements. If you cannot work, then give your love to people.
10-07:
You cannot avoid accepting God's existence, for nothing exists without Him. 

As Epictetus said, "All I know I know because there is God, and I know Him because He gives me the knowledge of everything." Lucy Mallory adds, "God is not an idol; he is an ideal which we should strive for in our everyday life." Remembering God is essential, not just with words, but by being aware of His presence guiding and evaluating our actions.
10-08:
Only people who have never thought about life’s most important issues can believe that everything is possible for the human intellect. There are people who believe in those things which they understand with their hearts, and this group is the wisest and most resolute.

"All beginnings are mysteries, the mystery of creation." —HENRI AMIEL

"Contemporary science cannot teach you about God and His virtues. The sciences cannot make you virtuous, but they help you on your way." —LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

Do not pretend to understand something that you do not.
10-09:
A person who comes into an understanding of the life of his spiritual self cannot fear evil, either in life or in death. 

That which is born of the flesh is flesh; and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit. —JOHN 3:6

The souls of wise people look to the future state of their existence; all of their thoughts are concentrated toward eternity. —MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO

Evil cannot touch a person who knows his spirituality.
10-10:
A man is both an animal and a spiritual being at the same time. As an animal, a man is afraid of death; as a spiritual being he does not experience death. 

In the last moments before death, the soul leaves the body and unites with the limitless, timeless, and eternal soul. Death marks a change, it marks the disappearance of the dwelling place of your conscience, but the conscience itself cannot be destroyed by death. 

Perhaps you fear the changes that death will bring, but a similar great change already happened at the time of your birth, and nothing bad came out of it.
10-11:
Most people are proud of unnecessary or even harmful things: fame, power, and wealth. 

As the saying goes, "A person who loves himself has the advantage of having very few competitors." Additionally, "There is no wisdom in he who thinks that he is wise." A selfish person is limited, and this limitation is deeply connected to their selfishness. 

Ultimately, a proud person may initially command respect, but when their influence fades, they become the object of jokes, revealing the emptiness of their pride.
10-12:
Any departure from accepted traditions and customs requires a large and serious effort, but true understanding of new things always requires such an effort. 

—LUCY MALORY

You should behave as you think is good, but not following the advice of the crowd. 
—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

It is bad to irritate people by stepping back from their customs and traditions, but it is even worse to deny the requirements of your conscience and intellect by following the customs of the crowd.
10-13:
A state system, no matter what kind of state system it is, functions at a far remove from the requirements of Christianity.

In those countries where wise people are in power, their subjects do not notice the existence of their rulers.
—LAO-TZU

State violence cannot be destroyed by decree, only by truth and love. You should live in such a way that violence is not necessary for you.
10-14:
People are taught to speak, but their major concern should be how to keep silent. 

If your tongue speaks good, there is nothing better in the world; if your tongue speaks evil, there is nothing worse. 
—The TALMUD

Let your tongue become accustomed to the words “I do not know.” 
—EASTERN WISDOM

Keep silent, for you will never regret that you have kept silently but you will often regret that you spoke too much.
10-15:
Your chief task in life is the care of your soul. You should care for your soul and work to improve it, and you can improve it only with love. The meaning of life lies in two major areas: your personal perfection and service to other people. 

As you move toward perfection, you transition from a material to a spiritual plane, a plane of goodness, without time or death. From childhood to death, humans should strive to grow, to get closer and closer to the spiritual life, and to learn what God wants, and your life will become filled with freedom and joy.
10-16:
God is in all of us, and it is possible for all of us to find and understand him there. To know yourself is to discover the good that lies within. 

—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA
"A person cannot be good without God."

If you are going through a hard time, work harder to understand God; as soon as you understand Him, all difficult things will become easy, and you will feel love and joy. If a person does not feel a divine force within himself, this does not mean that a divine force does not exist in him, but that he has not yet learned how to recognize it.
10-17:
If there are people and there is God, then there are relationships between God and people. These relationships are changing with time, and mankind’s religious conceptions are constantly evolving. 

God is spirit and part of this spirit lives inside me and gives sense to my life. 

There is much good to be learned from various religious texts, but the closer a religious thinker is to us in time, the more they can help us apply these teachings to our present-day lives.
10-18:
The past no longer exists; the future has not yet come; there is only the present. And only in the present can the divine nature of the free human soul be manifested.

Then Jesus said unto them, Yet a little while is the light with you. Walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you: for he that walketh in darkness knoweth not whither he goeth.
—JOHN 12:35

If you become angry, you must know that you not only perform evil, but you also create an evil habit, and you increase your potential for further evil.
—EPICTETUS 

If you want to do a good deed, do it now. The time will pass, and you will not have the chance again.
10-19:
The meaning of life is revealed to those who are ready to accept the things which will be revealed. 

"If a person knows what he should do, he will understand everything which he should know," as the philosopher Lichtenberg implies through his questions. Every bird knows where to make her nest, and thus knows her purpose in life, notes Chinese Wisdom. The real meaning of life is simple, yet only possible to grasp when looking inward as an individual, rather than seeking a universal meaning.
10-20:
The life of man is filled with intellect only when the fulfillment of your duty is understood. 

As Henry George notes, "We all know for sure that death waits for us. We do not know when, just as we do not know where we came from." 

Indian Wisdom adds, "Virtue is a service which a person should do for himself. If there were no heaven and no God who ruled this world, even then virtue would be the necessary law of life." 

Ultimately, the key to a fulfilling life is to "look for a kind life which is in harmony with the will of God, and then you will fulfill the duty of your life."
10-21:
In the same way as the storm troubles and muddies the waters, so too passions trouble our souls and interfere with our understanding of this life.

"People with great and wonderful souls are always quiet and happy. Those people who do not have spirituality are always unhappy." —CHINESE PROVERB
"Do not be concerned too much with what will happen. Everything which happens will be good and useful for you." —EPICTETUS
"A person will understand his place in the world only when he understands his soul." —CHINESE WISDOM

Real power is not in momentary desires, but in complete calmness, allowing for useful thoughts to appear and guide us in life.
10-22:
To stop your tongue from talking too much is a sign of great virtue. 

"The human soul, not by itself, but by some power, is pushed closer and closer to truth and goodness, and the better we understand this, the more humble we will be." —MARCUS AURELIUS

If you must judge your neighbor, do so directly and without creating animosity.
10-23:
Conscience is the understanding of the divine beginning which lives in us. As Jean Jacques Rousseau notes, "Conscience is the real judge between good and bad, conscience is what makes a person similar to God, and conscience is the greatest advantage of human nature." 

Marcus Aurelius advises, "The purpose of your life is not to do as the majority does, but to live according to the inner law which you understand in yourself. Do not act against your conscience or against truth." Ultimately, we must submit to the rules of conscience, for they are the rules of God.
10-24:
If the foundations of our life were not of the same nature in all of us, then we would not be able to explain to each other those feelings of compassion which we really experience. 

Compassion expressed in response to rage is the same as water for fire. 
—ARTHUR SCHOPENHAUER

Real compassion begins only when you put yourself in the place of those who suffer, and you feel real suffering.
10-25:
A person can understand his purpose in the same way as he understands his dignity, a notion only a religious person can grasp. 

As Muslih-ud-Din Saadi said, "Yes, I think about you, when I forget about God." This perspective underscores the importance of spiritual awareness in understanding our purpose and the value of others.

We fulfill the law of God when we feel the lives of others as our own life, as Giuseppe Mazzini noted. This sense of unity and respect for the spiritual dignity of others is rooted in self-awareness and freedom, allowing us to act in a manner worthy of our spiritual nature.
10-26:
For the moral, spiritual life, the importance of things is measured not by their material value, but by their level of goodness. 

A person understands himself not through thoughts, but with actions. It is only through making an effort that a person will understand his worth.
—JOHANN WOLFGANG VON GOETHE

To move yourself from the material to the spiritual means to do only spiritual things. 
—After contemplation on spiritual growth
10-27:
Real religion cannot be opposed to intellect. The force of our intellect must support the foundations of every real faith. 

—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

If God, the object of our faith, is higher than our understanding, and if you do not understand Him, it does not mean that you should not use your intellect trying to understand Him.

—FYODOR STRAKHOV

In order to understand the truth, you should not suppress your intellect, but rather purify it, exercise it, and intellectually try and test everything which we can possibly put to the test.
10-28:
Pain is the necessary condition of our body, and suffering is the necessary condition of our spiritual life, from birth to death. 

—MARCUS AURELIUS

You should welcome everything which happens to you from birth to death, because the existence and the purpose of the world is in these cases.

—MARCUS AURELIUS

Only in the storm can you see the art of the real sailor; only on the battlefield can you see the bravery of a soldier. 

—DANIEL ACHINSKY
10-29:
There is nothing more important than an example, as it leads us to do good deeds that would be impossible without it. 

"The contrary is also true" - this simple statement highlights the destructive power of negative examples. As the Dhammapada warns, "Those who do not think independently are under the influence of somebody else who thinks for them." 

Ultimately, "Evil influence can be destroyed only by good influence," emphasizing the importance of surrounding ourselves with positive examples to cultivate a good life.
10-30:
A sense of self-importance that surpasses all limits is a mental illness called mania grandiosa. 

As it's said, "sacrifice gives us complete freedom, and frees us from ourselves, from the slavery of our dissipation." Our passions can enslave us, but self-sacrifice can free us from this tyranny. Teaching people goodness is done for the purpose of self-sacrifice and service to others, as exemplified by Christ, who "gave his life on the cross for a great purpose, not in vain."
10-31:
Nothing can interfere with the growth of truth in the world: nothing except the wish to save old prejudices. 

"People say that God created mankind after his image. This means that man created God after his image." 
—GEORGE LICHTENBERG

Disrespect for traditions has not caused even one thousandth part of the great evil which was caused by the old prejudices, traditions, customs, and institutions which should not exist at present.
11-01:
A man who considers himself the master of his own life can never be humble, because he thinks that he has no obligation to anyone. In contrast, the man who considers service to God to be the purpose of his life is always humble, because he feels that he has never fulfilled his obligations.

As the Lord said, "So likewise ye, when ye shall have done all those things which are commanded you, say, We are unprofitable servants: we have done that which was our duty to do" (Luke 17:5-10). This mindset is echoed in the words of Thomas à Kempis, "The better a person knows himself, the smaller and less significant he seems to himself and the higher he elevates himself toward God." Truly kind people also embody this humility, as noted in Chinese Wisdom, "Truly kind people forget the good things they have done in the past."
11-02:
A deed done only to achieve glory is always bad, no matter what consequences it has. 
As Blaise Pascal notes, "We are not satisfied with our real life. We want to live an imaginary life, a life in which we seem different in the eyes of other people than we are in reality." 
Additionally, "Let the judgments of others be the consequence of your deeds, not their purpose." 
To live only for God, you should do good works which no one will ever discover, and in doing so, you will experience a special joy.
11-03:
The law of God is the only real law, valid for all mankind, and human law can only be valid when in harmony with it. 

As GIUSEPPE MAZZINI notes, "Those human laws are good which are based on the law of God. Those human laws are bad which contradict the laws of God; we should change these laws." 

To align with the law of God, we must overcome the prejudices and lies of previous centuries and replace existing laws with the law of God, as the current contradiction between human and divine law only grows stronger.
11-04:
Debates conceal the truth more often than they reveal it. Real truth should be revealed in solitude. 

"He has power who can keep silent in an argument, even though he is right." 
"You should abstain from arguments. They are very illogical ways to convince people. Opinions are like nails: the stronger you hit them, the deeper inside they go." —DECIMUS JUNIUS JUVENALIS
The more urgently you want to speak, the more likely it is that you will say something foolish.
11-05:
Thought is the glorification of truth; therefore, bad thoughts are those which have not been thought through to the end. 

So too will bad thoughts drop shadows on the white surface of your soul. 

—INDIAN WISDOM

You cannot send away a bad thought when it appears in your mind, but you can create other thoughts which will weaken or destroy this bad thought. For example, when I concentrate on the thought that criticizing another person is bad because I am not perfect, and he has the same God within him as I have within me, then I cannot stop loving this person. 

Meditation is the way to eternity; lightheartedness is the way to death. 

—BUDDHIST WISDOM
11-06:
Blaming your neighbor is harmful, both for you and for others. A story illustrates this: at a party, each guest was slandered as they left, until the last guest decided to stay, fearing the same treatment. 

As Chinese Wisdom advises, "Be strict to yourself and forgive others, and then you will have no enemies." Forgiveness is key to living in peace, as it allows us to let go of negativity and focus on our own actions. 

The power of words is also important, as "a word is an expression of thoughts; thoughts are the expression of divine force." This is why it's essential to use our words wisely, as an old man demonstrated by speaking slowly and carefully to avoid sinning with his words.
11-07:
You can look at life as death, and death as an awakening. 

I was dead before I was born, and at my death I will return to the same state, as George Lichtenberg noted. Marcus Tullius Cicero shared a similar perspective, saying, "When the end comes, I will leave my life in the same way, as if I leave an inn and not my home, because I think that my stay in this life is temporary and that death is only a transfer to another state." Cicero also found comfort in the idea of an eternal soul, stating, "Even if I err in saying that the soul is eternal, nevertheless I am happy that I made this mistake." Ultimately, our questions about death are misguided, for "when we die, we leave time behind."
11-08:
We come to a right understanding of our life in relation to God in the same way we understand our relation to the world and to its things. 

The truth is that if our heart understands God our intellect will then start to seek Him.
—GEORGE LICHTENBERG

The basic foundation of the understanding of God is inside of us.
—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

It's not about knowing the details of God's life, but about accepting Him as your master and knowing what He wants you to do. Every person understands God in different ways, but people fulfill His will in the same way.
11-09:
Self-admiration is the beginning of pride. 

As Blaise Pascal notes, "Those who do not hate their own selfishness, their placing of themselves higher than the rest of the world, are blind, because this action contradicts the truth." 

Pride can be likened to a substance that occupies less space the lighter it is, as Eastern Wisdom suggests, highlighting the emptiness of proud qualities. 

The path to perfection is hindered by pride and self-satisfaction, making it essential to recognize the need for self-improvement, rather than claiming to teach others.
11-10:
From the moment a religious gathering claimed the highest authority, a great lie originated, deceiving many and destroying millions. This lie has been perpetuated throughout history, as seen in the brutal punishment of Dr. Leyton in 1682, who was severely beaten and mutilated for criticizing a bishop in the name of Christianity.

—RANDALL THOMAS DAVIDSON

Christ's intention was not to establish external authorities, but to put the law of God into people's hearts, making them self-governed. 
—HERBERT NEWTON

If there is a true church, the people who are inside it cannot see it from the outside.
11-11:
Moral perfection is the impossible goal, but moving to it is the law of human life. 

As it's said, "Feel in your heart the kind of person you should be; this feeling will give you power." Outer consequences are not in our power to control; it is only possible to make an effort, and inner consequences always follow from our effort.
11-12:
The land is the general and equal possession of all humanity, and therefore cannot be the property of individuals.

As Black Hawk notes, "My intellect teaches me that land cannot be sold." This idea is echoed in Leviticus 25:23, "The land shall not be sold for ever: for the land is mine; for ye are strangers and sojourners with me." Immanuel Kant also emphasizes that "All people, from the first, and without any judicial act, should possess the earth." The possession of land as property is seen as a profound injustice, a crime that has become normalized in our world.
11-13:
Self-perfection should be one’s primary motivation. If you are truthful to yourself, you will never be satisfied with yourself. 

The first rule of achieving goodness is to think only about self-perfection, and do so without thought to being praised by others. 
—CHINESE WISDOM

He who moves from doing bad deeds to doing good deeds enlightens this world like the moon coming out from behind the clouds. 
—DHAMMAPADA, a book of BUDDHIST WISDOM

Dissatisfaction is a necessary condition of intellectual life, pushing you to work and strive for improvement.
11-14:
The most important knowledge is that which guides the way you lead your life. It is harmful to engage in meaningless intellectual activity when you do not wish to, as this can be detrimental to your well-being. 

As Lucius Annaeus Seneca notes, "Your will is not good until you have changed the habits of your intellect, and they will improve only when they follow the eternal laws of life." Additionally, Indian Wisdom reminds us that "Only those who have real knowledge know what to do." Ultimately, the quality of knowledge is what truly matters, not the quantity.
11-15:
The joy of wealth is inconstant. 

Wherever your heart is, there will be your treasure. The heart of a person whose major treasure is wealth is buried in filth.
—SAINT JOHN CHRYSOSTOM

Give this person the inner work of his intellect, and he will be happier than the richest man.
—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

For those who live a spiritual life, wealth is not only unnecessary but uncomfortable. It stops the development of one’s real life.
11-16:
Faith answers those questions to which the intellect cannot find answers, but which must be asked. 

As Immanuel Kant notes, "There is only one true religion, though there are many different faiths." Giuseppe Mazzini adds, "It is only faith which creates the powerful convictions, the energy, and the unity which can cure society." 

A universal spirit guides us, urging us to be closer to God and to each other, and when a person's faith is close to the truth, their life is happier.
11-17:
We suffer from the past, and we spoil our future because we neglect the present. The best the future can offer is dreams, and there is only one thing which really exists: the present. 

As Johann Wolfgang Von Goethe said, "Be attentive to the present. Only in the present time can we understand eternity." Additionally, the Talmud reminds us, "Use your body, your vessel, today; tomorrow it can be broken." Living in the present allows us to let go of anguish about the past and worries about the future, and instead, feel freedom and happiness.
11-18:
You cannot measure good by either the feeling of need or sacrifice, but only by the communication with God which is established between the giver and the receiver. 

—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA notes, "Goodness exists only in a proper, virtuous life." 

As —MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO states, "It is not a virtue, but a kind of deceitful similitude, to fulfill our duty for the purpose of its reward." 

True happiness is found in a life of continuous good deeds, as —MARCUS AURELIUS says, "I call this life a happy one in which I do one good deed after another, with no intervals between them." We do good only when we do not notice what we do, when we forget ourselves and live only in others.
11-19:
A material evil done by a person does not return to the evildoer, but the evil feeling created will fester in his soul and make him suffer. 

As Indian Wisdom notes, "If in the morning a person wants to do evil unto others, evil will return to him by nightfall." Saint Augustine also warns, "The evildoer harms himself first, before he harms others." The Dhammapada, a book of Buddhist Wisdom, further emphasizes the importance of self-control, stating, "Every man becomes what he teaches others to be... Every man has power only over himself." Ultimately, there is no material goodness that can restore the soul after the damage done by the evil one creates.
11-20:
Those who suffer through to the end will be saved. Very often a person becomes desperate or even stops in his purpose when only a small effort is needed to achieve it.

—MATTHEW 10:17-20 reminds us that when faced with adversity, "it shall be given you in that same hour what ye shall speak: for it is not ye that speak, but the Spirit of your Father which speaketh in you."

"Right cannot be defeated, because it is done not by your will but according to the eternal laws of God." —THOMAS CARLYLE

Ultimately, do not seek love in other people, and do not complain about the absence of their love for you. Instead, try to please God, rather than people.
11-21:
There is no deed in this life so impossible that you cannot do it. Your whole life should be lived as an heroic deed. 

Every time you wake up and ask yourself, “What good things am I going to do today?” remember that, when the sun goes down at sunset, it will take a part of your life with it. 
—the Indian Proverb

The virtue of a person is measured not by his outstanding efforts, but by his everyday behavior.
—the Blaise Pascal

All things, even the most complicated things, become simple and clear if you separate them from other people and place them before God for judgment. Other than our service to God, all our actions, whether we consider them important or not, are completely trivial.
11-22:
The more busy you are with the improvement of your inner life, the more active you become in social life, helping other people.

As Vladimir Chertkov notes, "It is as wrong for one person to rule many as it is for many to rule one." Thomas Carlyle also observes, "What is truth? For the majority of people, truth is the majority of counted votes." Henry David Thoreau's words offer a sense of liberation, "When I sit on the seashore and listen to the waves beating on the sand, I feel free from any obligation, and I think that all the people of the world can change their constitutions without me." Ultimately, spiritual growth is about harmony with eternal laws, as seen in the contrast between building and planting, where nature helps what is planted, causing growth in everything.
11-23:
The question of life's meaning becomes simple when a person asks, "What should I do?" 

As the path forward is uncertain, "your life should have a deep purpose, a significance that will not depend on whether it is short or long." 

In essence, "Our soul's perfection is our life's purpose; any other purpose, keeping death in mind, has no substance." This perspective underscores the importance of living a life of purpose and substance, rather than distraction and addiction.
11-24:
Helping others takes the form not only of physical aid, but of spiritual support for your neighbor. 

"Real generosity requires that you give this person a place in your heart." 
"He is virtuous who does not pay any attention to libel and evil speech." 
"You should be truthful; you should avoid wrath; you should give to those who ask, because they ask for small things."

With humbleness, kindness, and self-sacrifice, you will take the weapon from any enemy. Try not to hide the shameful memories of your sins, but remember them before you judge your neighbor.
11-25:
War is murder. People of planet Earth are on a very low level of development, as evidenced by the constant preparations for war and mass manslaughter. 

As Camille Flammarion notes, "People do not understand that the life of each man is his own personal private property." Until people reject the power of government to govern, war will persist as its consequence.
11-26:
One heart illuminates another heart and can illuminate thousands of other hearts, just as one candle lights another. 

"Beware of those who want to convince you that it is impossible to strive for good just because it is impossible to reach perfection." 
—JOHN RUSKIN

The strongest influence is the example of a good life, becoming a blessing for people who see, know, and understand it. 
"It is difficult to bring people to goodness with lessons, but it is easy to do so by example." 
—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

Seek good company and avoid those that harm your soul.
11-27:
If a passion embraces you, remember that this desire is not a part of your soul, but a dark covering that shuts out its true qualities.

"You should be a lantern for yourself. Draw close to the light within you and seek no other shelter." —BUDDHIST WISDOM

The soul is a mirror reflecting the divine intellect, and its light illuminates everything around you. As soon as you feel passion, try to understand your divine nature and fight against passions that possess you.
11-28:
Life is not destroyed, only changed, by death. 

As Ralph Waldo Emerson notes, "What is important is not the length of life, but the depth of life. What is most important is not to make life longer, but to take your soul out of time, as every sublime act does." The level of fear you feel about death is the level of your understanding of life. The less you fear death, the more you possess freedom, tranquillity, and an understanding of the greatness of your spirit and the joy of life. Faith in eternity is given to us not in meditation, but in life, and the understanding of eternity is part of the nature of the human soul.
11-29:
A word is an action. 

Do not say words you do not feel, lest your soul be blackened with darkness. 
—From the BOOK OF DIVINE THOUGHTS

Your enemies can be more useful to you than your friends, because your friends can often pardon your weaknesses, but your enemies notice them and attract your attention to them.
—CHINESE WISDOM

A person’s tongue is a tool which is sufficient to transmit the ideas created by the human mind. 
—LAJOS KOSSUTH

Never is there a single instance when a lie can be justified.
11-30:
When a humble person forgets himself, he is united with God. 

As the Talmud notes, "People who try to force circumstances become their slaves. Those who use them become their masters." Lao-Tzu also observes, "Weakness defeats strength, tenderness defeats rigidity... The weakest in the world gain victory over the strongest; therefore, there is a great advantage in humility and silence." Ultimately, "The more humble a person is, the freer and stronger he is."
12-01:
A woman's chief mission is to serve God, with her method of service being more specific, often through the birth and upbringing of new workers for the Lord. 

As the Talmud notes, "If a woman's kindness is boundless, then her rage can also have no limits. A good wife is the best gift for a husband, and an evil wife is a terrible ulcer for a man." 
Additionally, Gotthold Ephraim Lessing observes, "The more beautiful a woman is, the more honest she should be because it is only through honesty that she can deal with all the evil and damage which her beauty can produce." 
Self-sacrifice is also inherent to a woman's nature, with selfishness being particularly unbecoming.
12-02:
“Do not kill”: these words refer not just to the killing of a person, but to the killing of anything alive. 

Most people would rather stop eating meat than kill these animals themselves, revealing an inherent compassion and disgust at killing sheep, cows, or chickens.

—JOHN RUSKIN
"The stupidity, lawlessness, and harm, both physical and moral, of meat eating is so clear that meat eating is not supported by reasoning but by old traditions and prejudices."
12-03:
Art is the activity whereby a person makes a conscious attempt to use the particular means at his disposal to transmit his feelings to others so that they feel them as intensely as he does. 

As Emerson said, music helps people to find the greatness in their souls, a concept that can be applied to any form of art. A new work of art should bring a new emotion in our life, unifying the artist, the work, and the audience in a shared experience.
12-04:
The essence of divine law is to love God and your neighbor, a law that can be fulfilled even in solitude, as God exists always. 

As Epictetus says, "I do not grieve that a person dies, that he loses his money, his estate, or all his property, all that can belong to a man. But it is a great pity when a person loses his greatest possession, his human dignity." 

We must also heed Ralph Waldo Emerson's words: "Do not do anything, either among others or alone, which is opposed by your conscience." Ultimately, every person has an obligation to himself, as well as to his neighbor, to respect the human being within.
12-05: The longer mankind exists, the more liberated it becomes from prejudice and the more clearly it perceives the law of life. As Immanuel Kant notes, "Religion and law try to escape from criticism, religion by saying that it is divine and law by showing that it is powerful. But some suspicions arise from this escape, because we can respect only those things which stand up in free and public trial." This sentiment is echoed by Pythagoras, who advises, "Study everything, and put intellect first." By embracing intellectual curiosity, we can fearlessly question old stories and legends, trusting that "the pure intellect cannot destroy anything without replacing it with the truth."
12-06:
We have misconceptions not because we think illogically but because we live our lives badly.

As Jean Jacques Rousseau notes, "Ignorance cannot lead to evil, misconceptions lead to evil: it’s not what people do know, it’s what they pretend that they do." Arthur Schopenhauer also warns that "Every misconception is a poison: there are no harmless misconceptions." The fight between our spiritual and material inner nature leads to misconceptions, and freeing others from them is a profound act of charity, as it is a deed that can bring great liberation.
12-07:
Life consists of numerous invisible, imperceptible changes, changes that began at birth and end with death. 

Truly, truly, I say to you, unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains by itself alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. 
—JOHN 12:24-25

Why are you so afraid of change? Nothing in this world can be done without change. 
—MARCUS AURELIUS

Everything in this world blooms, grows, and returns to its roots. Returning to one’s roots means becoming united with nature; becoming united with nature involves eternity. 
—LAO-TZU
12-08:
It seems so easy to fulfill the law of God, but we are still far from this fulfillment. 

The law of right living is not immediately clear to the wise man, but it becomes much clearer when he follows it. 
—CONFUCIUS

There is only one eternal law, it does not change and it governs all people at all times.
—MARCUS TULLIUS CICERO
12-09:
Man’s purpose is to serve all mankind, not to serve only one man while doing harm to others. 

"Patriotism is not a virtue. Sacrificing your life for an institution which embodies prejudice cannot be your duty." 

Loving your country and loving your family are both virtues that can become vices when they become overwhelming and damage your love for your neighbor.
12-10:
One of the worst things possible is to follow the rule: “Be like everyone, follow the crowd.” 

As Matthew warns, "Woe to the world because of its stumbling block! For it is inevitable that stumbling blocks come; but woe to that man through whom the stumbling block comes!" —MATTHEW 18:7-9

Levi, Peter, and Paul all left their jobs to pursue a greater calling, as John Ruskin notes. False shame is the devil’s favorite emotion; it is even worse than false pride, for "Pride can support evil, but false shame stops goodness." —JOHN RUSKIN

Ultimately, "There is no evil in the world itself. All evil exists in our souls, and can be destroyed."
12-11:
Nothing is more joyful than the labor of a farmer tilling the soil. 

The best food is the food you or your children make by yourself. 
—MOHAMMED

Those who provide for their own food by themselves deserve more respect than those who claim to be religious.
—The TALMUD
12-12:
Kindness defeats everything and can never be defeated. 

You can stand firm in a fight against everything except kindness.
—JEAN JACQUES ROUSSEAU

If a good action has an ulterior cause, then it is not truly good. 
The most tender plants can push their way through the hardest rocks, and it is the same with kindness. Nothing can stop a truly kind and sincere person.
—HENRY DAVID THOREAU

The person does not exist to whom you cannot do good.
—LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA

The best and easiest way to thwart evil in this world is to respond to it with kind words, return an evil action with good.
12-13: True faith is faith only if the actions of your life are in harmony with it and never contradict it. As the Indian Proverb says, "During the day you should behave in such a way that you can sleep at night in peace; and in your youth you should behave in such a way that you can live in your old age in peace." Lao-Tzu notes, "Those who have weak faith themselves cannot arouse faith in others." Ultimately, faith is about living a life of integrity and conviction, as reflected in the idea that "We can never fully understand the final purpose of human life," but we can trust that our actions are working towards something good and beautiful.
12-14:
A person who becomes close to God understands that God lives within him. As Angelus, a mystical poet, said, “I see God with the same eye with which He sees me.” 

The soul is the lamp of God, and people live in Him and He in them. If you believe in God, you know that you need not wish for anything because the wishes of God always come true; and you need never be afraid of anything, because God fears nothing. 

As Heraclitus noted, the nature of a soul is mysterious and cannot be defined. Ultimately, understanding your unification with God brings freedom from unhappiness, regardless of circumstances.
12-15:
Only misconceptions need to be supported by elaborate arguments. Truth can always stand alone.

"All goodness is as nothing compared to the goodness of truth; all sweets are as nothing compared to the sweetness of truth. The bliss of truth surpasses all other joys in the world." —BUDDHIST WISDOM

Misconceptions exist only for a finite period of time, but real truth remains as it always was. "Lies are always harmful to all living things." No person is without sin; no person is completely truthful, yet one can strive to move toward truth and virtue.
12-16:
Only the spread of love between people can improve the existing social structure. Love gives meaning to human life. 

If you raise people to love the feeling of love, they will start to live in love.
—MEE-TEE, a student of CONFUCIUS

Only one clear quality marks an action as either good or evil: If it increases the amount of love in the world, it is good. The time is coming, a time of concord, harmony, forgiveness, and love.
12-17:
Our understanding of ourselves as discrete beings separate from each other grows out of conditions of our life in time and space. The less we feel this separation, the more we feel our unification with all other living creatures, the lighter our load, and the more joyful our life will be.

"For the body is not one member, but many…. And if one member suffers, all the members suffer with it; if one member is honored, all the members rejoice with it." —I CORINTHIANS 12:14,26

"A small branch cut off from a big limb is separated from the whole tree. In the same way when a person is in an argument with another person, he is separated from all humanity." —MARCUS AURELIUS

We manifest in love our understanding of the unity of our being with others, and in so doing we make our life greater. The more we love, the wider, larger, and more joyful our life becomes.
12-18:
Mankind moves ceaselessly toward perfection through the efforts of individuals striving for personal perfection, ultimately creating the kingdom of God. 

As F. Robert de Lamennais says, "Do not be embarrassed by all your bad deeds, by all your misfortunes. Do not be embarrassed, but fight ceaselessly, bravely, and without hesitation, because the kingdom of God will come." 

Life has no meaning without purpose, notes Giuseppe Mazzini, emphasizing that to lack purpose is to deny the existence of God and render life an "evil, stupid joke." 

Ultimately, it is through submission to God and obedience to His laws that we can understand His will, as John Ruskin suggests. Every person should make an effort, no matter how small, to improve humanity's well-being.
12-19:
Real goodness is in your hands. It follows a good person as a shadow follows its object. 

As Epictetus notes, "Everything which can make us better and happier was given to us by God. If our conscience is clear, then nothing can harm us." Blaise Pascal also observes, "They who have decided to dedicate their lives to spiritual perfection will never be dissatisfied or unhappy, because all that they want is in their power." Benedictus Spinoza adds, "Happiness, pure happiness, is a virtue in itself."
12-20:
The real purpose of Christianity is obvious to all, and it is stronger than the lies which try to pervert it. To understand its true meaning, we must separate the religion taught by Jesus from the religion whose subject is Jesus. 

As Henri Amiel notes, this distinction is crucial. Giuseppe Mazzini also echoes this sentiment, saying, "In different ways and in different tongues, all humanity repeats the words of the Lord’s prayer, “Let thy kingdom come on earth as it is in heaven.” Ultimately, the kingdom of God is coming closer, and though we may not see it personally, its arrival is imminent and inevitable.
12-21:
At the highest level of consciousness, an individual is alone. Wise people remain at this high point, with the help of prayer. 

As Blaise Pascal notes, a person in extreme suffering or solitude can only persevere through prayer. 

One can live without prayer either when he is possessed by his passions or when his life is completely dedicated to God. But for a person who is fighting his passion and yet far from fulfilling his duty, prayer is a necessary condition of life. 

—BLAISE PASCAL
12-22:
The notion that improving civilization can be achieved by changing its outer forms is false, drawing people away from efforts that truly improve lives. Civilization is first of all a moral thing, with morality being the basis of civilization. 

As Henri Amiel notes, "Civilization is first of all a moral thing. Without truth, respect for duty, love of neighbor, virtue, everything is destroyed." Edmund Yates adds, "The proper direction of man’s thought is not toward the creation of new laws for government, but toward the acceptance of every person’s moral dignity." Giuseppe Mazzini emphasizes, "We should, first of all, understand that we are all children of the same father, and we should fulfill the same general law: live not for ourselves, but to help others be happy."
12-23:
Wisdom is understanding how eternal truth can be applied to life. Socrates brought philosophy to the people, encouraging them to study the science of life and human morals. 

A wise person knows few things, but all that he knows is necessary both for humanity and for himself. As Marcus Tullius Cicero said, "He who understands his soul will understand the divine spark within himself." Lao-Tzu also noted, "Wise people cannot be sufficiently educated, and educated people cannot be sufficiently wise." The value of wisdom is unparalleled, comparable to a vessel of water in a desert, surpassing all other knowledge.
12-24:
As we age, our spiritual power grows while our physical power diminishes. 

Older people who live spiritual lives ever widen their spiritual horizons, and ever expand their consciences, notes the TALMUD. One becomes older in order to become kinder, observes JOHANN WOLFGANG VON GOETHE, adding that with age, one has already made all the mistakes there are to make. The growth and development of the soul is more important than power and glory, according to HENRI AMIEL. Understanding and living with one's spiritual being brings a joy that can never be destroyed, and always grows.
12-25: Kindness and virtue come from the heart, and should be performed without thought for the opinion of others, or of future rewards. As charity is considered, it's essential to remember that "Charity is good only when it comes as a sacrifice; only then do those who receive the material gift receive the spiritual gift too." Virtue and charity start at home, and if it's not a genuine sacrifice, it can only irritate those who receive it, rather than providing true benefit.
12-26:
Childhood is the period in which underlying convictions are formed. 

"Talking and reasoning does not even have one thousandth the influence a true example has." —GUSTAV STRUVE

"The strong desire for a pleasant and ideal life is a child’s worst misfortune. It is crucial that children should know how to work from an early age." —IMMANUEL KANT

It is essential to teach children kindness and simplicity in life and work, supported by your own good example, as this will educate them on the importance of living virtuously.
12-27:
All that we see and know does not exist in reality, because it is the product of our limited ability to understand reality. 
"Instead of saying that the world is reflected in our intellect, better to say that our intellect is reflected in the world." 
—GEORGE LICHTENBERG

Only the teachings of kindness are never ruined or affected by age. 
In order to understand the true essence of a thing we must transform the physical into the spiritual.
12-28:
Science is vitally important when it is harnessed to reveal the law of human life. 
"The universe is limitless, and impossible for anyone to understand. Therefore, we cannot completely understand the life of our own body." —BLAISE PASCAL
“Science” is not quite the concept which people use this word to identify; it is the highest, most important, most necessary object of our understanding.
12-29:
As long as there is violence, there will be war. One cannot defeat violence with more violence, only with nonresistance to and nonparticipation in it.

—"LETURNO"

The wild instinct of military murder has developed in humanity over the course of thousands of years, and has become rooted in our brains. 
—"FREDERICK II" is quoted as saying "If my soldiers started thinking, not a single soldier would remain in my army." 
Wars can be stopped only if people are ready to be persecuted for not participating in them.
12-30:
The understanding of our spiritual fraternity is rooted in the knowledge that we originated from one divine Father, carrying His image within us. As William Ellery Channing notes, this realization reveals that "there is the same divine life in all people, as well as in ourselves," creating a natural bond among people.

—WILLIAM ELLERY CHANNING

This unity is essential for love and harmony, as Marcus Tullius Cicero emphasizes, "We can love neither those whom we fear nor those who fear us." True understanding of our unity with all humanity comes from recognizing the divine beginning in us all, and is fostered by true religion, unobstructed by prejudices of state, nation, and class.
12-31:
The past does not exist. The future has not begun. The present is an infinitely small point in time in which the already nonexistent past meets the imminent future. 

“Time passes by!” we say. Time does not exist; only we move.

Time affects the body, but the life of the spirit has no meaning either in the past or in the future. All its life is concentrated in the present, as Marcus Aurelius notes. Divine spirit is eternal, it exists out of time, according to Henri Amiel. 

A person should concentrate all their spiritual force only on this present, for it is only in this present that our life occurs.
